Benchmark Results for gemini-2.5-flash-LowReasoning-Tools

Overall Summary

Test 1: Travelling Salesman Problem Solver (C++) Score: 5.00 / 21
Typical Prompt:
You are solving the Travelling Salesman Problem (TSP) in C++.

You must write a C++ solver that can handle ANY number of cities from trivial to ludicrous scale:
- **Trivial**: 10-40 cities (brute-force feasible for smallest cases)
- **Medium**: 100-1000 cities (requires heuristics)
- **Large**: 5000-100000 cities (requires efficient heuristics)
- **Extreme**: 500000-5000000 cities (requires very fast approximations)
- **Epic**: 10000000+ cities (requires highly optimized approximations)

**The Challenge:**
Your program will be tested with city counts ranging from 10 to 10000000. The same algorithm must work efficiently across ALL scales.

**Input format (stdin):**
Line 1: N (number of cities)
Lines 2..N+1: x y coordinates (floats)

**Output format (stdout):**
One line with N integers: the visit order of city indices (0-indexed), starting at city 0.
Do NOT include returning to city 0 (return is implicit).

**Critical Requirements:**
1. **Scalability**: Your algorithm must adapt based on problem size
2. **Performance**: Must complete within 300 seconds even for 1000,000,000 cities
3. **Quality**: Should produce reasonable routes at all scales
4. **Correctness**: Output must be a valid permutation of 0..N-1 starting with 0

**Environment:**
Platform: Windows 11 (build 10.0.26200)
Architecture: AMD64
CPU: AMD64 Family 23 Model 8 Stepping 2, AuthenticAMD
Core count: 64
Memory: 127.87 GB
SIMD: AVX, AVX2, SSE, SSE2, SSE3, SSE4.1, SSE4.2, SSSE3
SIMD detail: AVX=yes, AVX-512F=no, AVX2=yes, SSE=yes, SSE2=yes, SSE3=yes, SSE4.1=yes, SSE4.2=yes, SSSE3=yes

**C++ Compiler:**
MSVC cl 19.50.35730 | std=c++20 (/std:c++20)

Be sure that any deviation from the C++ standard library is supported by the given compiler,
as referencing the wrong intrinsics or non-standard header like 'bits/stdc++.h' could fail your submission.

Write complete, compilable C++ code with a main() function that reads stdin and writes stdout.
Include adaptive logic that chooses different strategies based on N.
 Prompt Changes:
Prompt parameters change between subpasses (increasing difficulty)
Subpass 0
Parameters: 10 Cities
View exact prompt
View raw AI output
View chain of thought

Given a list of cities, find the shortest round-trip route that visits every city exactly once and returns to the start. This is one of the most famous hard problems in computer science — checking every possible route is only feasible for a handful of cities, so the AI must use clever shortcuts to find a good route quickly.

Subpasses scale from 10 cities up to 1 000, so brute-force is impossible for the larger cases. The AI's route length is compared against a simple baseline; beating it earns full marks.

 1.0000
[10 cities] Route distance: 2449.07, Baseline: 2922.93, Ratio: 0.84x, Time: 0.0s - excellent (within 10% of baseline)

TSP Solver - 10 Cities

View Code (7128 chars)
#include <iostream>
#include <vector>
#include <cmath>
#include <limits>
#include <algorithm>
#include <numeric>

// Structure to represent a city
struct City {
    double x, y;
    int id;
};

// Function to calculate Euclidean distance between two cities
double calculateDistance(const City& c1, const City& c2) {
    return std::sqrt(std::pow(c1.x - c2.x, 2) + std::pow(c1.y - c2.y, 2));
}

// Nearest Neighbor algorithm to find an initial tour (with distMatrix)
std::vector<int> nearestNeighbor(int N, const std::vector<City>& cities, const std::vector<std::vector<double>>& distMatrix) {
    std::vector<int> tour;
    std::vector<bool> visited(N, false);

    // Start at city 0
    int currentCity = 0;
    tour.push_back(currentCity);
    visited[currentCity] = true;

    for (int i = 1; i < N; ++i) {
        double minDistance = std::numeric_limits<double>::max();
        int nextCity = -1;

        for (int j = 0; j < N; ++j) {
            if (!visited[j]) {
                if (distMatrix[currentCity][j] < minDistance) {
                    minDistance = distMatrix[currentCity][j];
                    nextCity = j;
                }
            }
        }
        currentCity = nextCity;
        tour.push_back(currentCity);
        visited[currentCity] = true;
    }
    return tour;
}

// Nearest Neighbor algorithm to find an initial tour (without distMatrix - calculates distances on the fly)
std::vector<int> nearestNeighbor(int N, const std::vector<City>& cities) {
    std::vector<int> tour;
    std::vector<bool> visited(N, false);

    // Start at city 0
    int currentCity = 0;
    tour.push_back(currentCity);
    visited[currentCity] = true;

    for (int i = 1; i < N; ++i) {
        double minDistance = std::numeric_limits<double>::max();
        int nextCity = -1;

        for (int j = 0; j < N; ++j) {
            if (!visited[j]) {
                double dist = calculateDistance(cities[currentCity], cities[j]);
                if (dist < minDistance) {
                    minDistance = dist;
                    nextCity = j;
                }
            }
        }
        currentCity = nextCity;
        tour.push_back(currentCity);
        visited[currentCity] = true;
    }
    return tour;
}

// Calculate total tour length (with distMatrix)
double calculateTourLength(const std::vector<int>& tour, const std::vector<std::vector<double>>& distMatrix) {
    double totalLength = 0;
    for (size_t i = 0; i < tour.size() - 1; ++i) {
        totalLength += distMatrix[tour[i]][tour[i+1]];
    }
    totalLength += distMatrix[tour.back()][tour[0]]; // Return to start
    return totalLength;
}

// Calculate total tour length (without distMatrix - calculates distances on the fly)
double calculateTourLength(const std::vector<int>& tour, const std::vector<City>& cities) {
    double totalLength = 0;
    for (size_t i = 0; i < tour.size() - 1; ++i) {
        totalLength += calculateDistance(cities[tour[i]], cities[tour[i+1]]);
    }
    totalLength += calculateDistance(cities[tour.back()], cities[tour[0]]); // Return to start
    return totalLength;
}

// 2-opt optimization
std::vector<int> twoOpt(int N, std::vector<int> tour, const std::vector<std::vector<double>>& distMatrix) {
    bool improved = true;
    while (improved) {
        improved = false;
        for (int i = 1; i < N - 1; ++i) {
            for (int k = i + 1; k < N; ++k) {
                // Current edges: (tour[i-1], tour[i]) and (tour[k], tour[(k+1)%N])
                // New edges: (tour[i-1], tour[k]) and (tour[i], tour[(k+1)%N])

                int p1 = tour[i-1];
                int p2 = tour[i];
                int p3 = tour[k];
                int p4 = tour[(k + 1 == N) ? 0 : k + 1];

                double currentSegmentLength = distMatrix[p1][p2] + distMatrix[p3][p4];
                double newSegmentLength = distMatrix[p1][p3] + distMatrix[p2][p4];

                if (newSegmentLength < currentSegmentLength) {
                    std::reverse(tour.begin() + i, tour.begin() + k + 1);
                    improved = true;
                }
            }
        }
    }
    return tour;
}

// Main solving function
std::vector<int> solveTSP(int N, const std::vector<City>& cities) {
    std::vector<int> tour;

    if (N <= 15) { // Brute force for optimal solution up to N=15
        std::vector<std::vector<double>> distMatrix(N, std::vector<double>(N));
        for (int i = 0; i < N; ++i) {
            for (int j = 0; j < N; ++j) {
                distMatrix[i][j] = calculateDistance(cities[i], cities[j]);
            }
        }

        std::vector<int> p(N);
        std::iota(p.begin(), p.end(), 0); // Fills p with 0, 1, ..., N-1

        double minTourLength = std::numeric_limits<double>::max();
        std::vector<int> bestTour;

        std::vector<int> currentPermutation(N);
        currentPermutation[0] = 0; // Fix city 0 as the starting point

        std::vector<int> citiesToPermute;
        for (int i = 1; i < N; ++i) {
            citiesToPermute.push_back(i);
        }

        std::sort(citiesToPermute.begin(), citiesToPermute.end()); // Ensure starting with smallest permutation

        do {
            for (int i = 0; i < citiesToPermute.size(); ++i) {
                currentPermutation[i + 1] = citiesToPermute[i];
            }

            double currentLength = calculateTourLength(currentPermutation, distMatrix);

            if (currentLength < minTourLength) {
                minTourLength = currentLength;
                bestTour = currentPermutation;
            }
        } while (std::next_permutation(citiesToPermute.begin(), citiesToPermute.end()));
        tour = bestTour;

    } else if (N <= 2000) { // Medium N: Nearest Neighbor + 2-opt with distMatrix
        std::vector<std::vector<double>> distMatrix(N, std::vector<double>(N));
        for (int i = 0; i < N; ++i) {
            for (int j = 0; j < N; ++j) {
                distMatrix[i][j] = calculateDistance(cities[i], cities[j]);
            }
        }
        tour = nearestNeighbor(N, cities, distMatrix);
        tour = twoOpt(N, tour, distMatrix);
    } else { // N > 2000: Nearest Neighbor without distMatrix (distances on the fly)
        // For extremely large N (e.g., 10,000,000 cities), the O(N^2) complexity of this
        // Nearest Neighbor implementation will be too slow. A k-d tree or similar
        // data structure would be required to find nearest neighbors in O(log N) time,
        // reducing the overall complexity to O(N log N).
        tour = nearestNeighbor(N, cities);
        // For very large N, 2-opt is too slow, so we don't apply it here.
    }

    return tour;
}

int main() {
    std::ios_base::sync_with_stdio(false);
    std::cin.tie(NULL);

    int N;
    std::cin >> N;

    std::vector<City> cities(N);
    for (int i = 0; i < N; ++i) {
        cities[i].id = i;
        std::cin >> cities[i].x >> cities[i].y;
    }

    std::vector<int> resultTour = solveTSP(N, cities);

    for (int i = 0; i < N; ++i) {
        std::cout << resultTour[i] << (i == N - 1 ? "" : " ");
    }
    std::cout << std::endl;

    return 0;
}

Test 2: Chinese Postman Problem Solver (C#) Score: 0.00 / 13
Typical Prompt:
You are solving the Chinese Postman Problem (Route Inspection Problem) in C#.

You must write a C# solver that can handle ANY graph size from trivial to ludicrous scale:
- **Trivial**: 6-20 nodes, 8-45 edges (small graphs, exact algorithms feasible)
- **Medium**: 35-200 nodes, 80-600 edges (requires efficient algorithms)
- **Large**: 500-1000 nodes, 2000-5000 edges (requires optimized implementations)
- **Extreme**: 5000-10000 nodes, 30000-80000 edges (requires very fast algorithms)
- **Epic**: 50000-100000 nodes, 500000-1000000 edges (requires highly optimized algorithms)

**The Challenge:**
Your program will be tested with graphs ranging from 6 nodes to 100000 nodes. The same function must work efficiently across ALL scales.

**Input format (stdin):**
Line 1: N M (number of nodes, number of edges)
Lines 2..M+1: u v w (edge endpoints, 0-indexed, weight integer)

**Output format (stdout):**
A sequence of node indices (whitespace-separated) representing a route that starts at node 0,
traverses EVERY edge at least once, and returns to node 0.
You may optionally prefix with a single integer L (route length). If present, it must match the number of nodes listed.

**Critical Requirements:**
1. **Scalability**: Your algorithm must adapt based on graph size and complexity
2. **Performance**: Must complete within 30 seconds even for large graphs
3. **Correctness**: Must traverse every edge at least once

**Environment:**
Platform: Windows 11 (build 10.0.26200)
Architecture: AMD64
CPU: AMD64 Family 23 Model 8 Stepping 2, AuthenticAMD
Core count: 64
Memory: 127.87 GB
SIMD: AVX, AVX2, SSE, SSE2, SSE3, SSE4.1, SSE4.2, SSSE3
SIMD detail: AVX=yes, AVX-512F=no, AVX2=yes, SSE=yes, SSE2=yes, SSE3=yes, SSE4.1=yes, SSE4.2=yes, SSSE3=yes

**C# Compiler:**

Write complete, compilable C# code with a static void Main method.

View chain of thought

Imagine a mail carrier who must walk along every street in a neighbourhood and return home. Some streets may need to be walked twice if the network doesn't allow a perfect loop — the goal is to minimise that extra walking.

The AI must figure out which streets (edges) to re-traverse and produce a complete route. Subpasses increase the graph size, making the problem harder to solve efficiently.

Chinese Postman Problem - 6 nodes, 8 edges

View Code (3266 chars)
using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;

namespace ChinesePostman
{
    public class Edge
    {
        public int U { get; } // Start vertex
        public int V { get; } // End vertex
        public int Weight { get; }
        public int Id { get; } // Unique identifier for the edge

        public Edge(int u, int v, int weight, int id)
        {
            U = u;
            V = v;
            Weight = weight;
            Id = id;
        }

        public override string ToString()
        {
            return $"(U: {U}, V: {V}, W: {Weight})";
        }
    }

    public class Graph
    {
        private Dictionary<int, List<Edge>> _adj;
        public int NumVertices { get; private set; }
        public List<Edge> AllEdges { get; private set; }

        public Graph(IEnumerable<Edge> edges)
        {
            _adj = new Dictionary<int, List<Edge>>();
            AllEdges = new List<Edge>(edges);

            foreach (var edge in AllEdges)
            {
                if (!_adj.ContainsKey(edge.U)) _adj[edge.U] = new List<Edge>();
                if (!_adj.ContainsKey(edge.V)) _adj[edge.V] = new List<Edge>();

                _adj[edge.U].Add(edge);
                // For an undirected graph, add the edge to both vertices
                // Create a 'reverse' edge for representation, but conceptually it's the same edge
                _adj[edge.V].Add(new Edge(edge.V, edge.U, edge.Weight, edge.Id)); // Note: Same ID
            }
            NumVertices = _adj.Keys.Count;
        }

        public List<Edge> GetEdges(int vertex)
        {
            return _adj.TryGetValue(vertex, out var edges) ? edges : new List<Edge>();
        }

        public Dictionary<int, int> GetVertexDegrees()
        {
            var degrees = new Dictionary<int, int>();
            foreach (var vertex in _adj.Keys)
            {
                // Each edge in the adjacency list counts as one "connection"
                // For undirected graph, we count how many times a vertex appears as U or V in AllEdges
                // Or simply count the entries in its adjacency list for the full adjacency list.
                // Since we added (V,U) for each (U,V), the count in _adj[vertex] is already the degree.
                degrees[vertex] = _adj[vertex].Count;
            }
            return degrees;
        }

        public List<int> GetOddDegreeVertices()
        {
            var degrees = GetVertexDegrees();
            return degrees.Where(pair => pair.Value % 2 != 0).Select(pair => pair.Key).ToList();
        }

        // Method to add new edges (duplicates) to make degrees even
        public void AddEdges(IEnumerable<Edge> edgesToAdd)
        {
            foreach (var edge in edgesToAdd)
            {
                AllEdges.Add(edge);
                if (!_adj.ContainsKey(edge.U)) _adj[edge.U] = new List<Edge>();
                if (!_adj.ContainsKey(edge.V)) _adj[edge.V] = new List<Edge>();

                _adj[edge.U].Add(edge);
                _adj[edge.V].Add(new Edge(edge.V, edge.U, edge.Weight, edge.Id));
            }
            // Update NumVertices if new vertices are added, though unlikely in this scenario
            NumVertices = _adj.Keys.Count;
        }
    }
}

Test 3: Graph Layout - Edge Crossing Minimization (Rust) Score: 0.00 / 16
Typical Prompt:
You are solving a Graph Layout problem (Edge Crossing Minimization) in Rust.

You must write a Rust solver that can handle ANY graph size from trivial to ludicrous scale:
- **Trivial**: 6-15 nodes, small trees and cycles (planar, 0 crossings achievable)
- **Medium**: 20-100 nodes, dense planar and non-planar graphs
- **Large**: 500-1000 nodes, random dense graphs (many crossings unavoidable)
- **Extreme**: 5000-10000 nodes, very large random graphs

**The Challenge:**
Your program will be tested with graphs ranging from 6 nodes to 200,000 nodes. The same solver must work efficiently across ALL scales.

**Input format (stdin):**
Line 1: N M (number of nodes, number of edges)
Lines 2..M+1: u v (undirected edge endpoints, 0-indexed)

**Output format (stdout):**
Output N lines, one per node, with: x y coordinates (floats).
You may optionally prefix with a single integer N on the first line.

**Critical Requirements:**
1. **Scalability**: Your algorithm must adapt based on graph size and density
2. **Performance**: Must complete within 30 seconds even for large graphs
3. **Quality**: Should produce reasonable layouts with minimal crossings
4. **Robustness**: Handle disconnected components, self-loops, and parallel edges gracefully

**Environment:**
Platform: Windows 11 (build 10.0.26200)
Architecture: AMD64
CPU: AMD64 Family 23 Model 8 Stepping 2, AuthenticAMD
Core count: 64
Memory: 127.87 GB
SIMD: AVX, AVX2, SSE, SSE2, SSE3, SSE4.1, SSE4.2, SSSE3
SIMD detail: AVX=yes, AVX-512F=no, AVX2=yes, SSE=yes, SSE2=yes, SSE3=yes, SSE4.1=yes, SSE4.2=yes, SSSE3=yes

**Rust Compiler:**
rustc 1.92.0 (ded5c06cf 2025-12-08) | edition=2024 | host: x86_64-pc-windows-msvc

Write complete, compilable Rust code with a main() function.

View chain of thought

Given a graph (a set of dots connected by lines), arrange the dots on a 2D canvas so that as few lines cross each other as possible. A clean, readable layout is the goal — tangled spaghetti is the enemy.

Minimising crossings is extremely hard in general. The AI must place nodes intelligently; the baseline simply puts them in a circle. Subpasses increase the number of nodes and edges.

Test 4: Regular Tetrahedron Packing in Polyhedra (C++) Score: 0.00 / 11
Typical Prompt:
You are solving a 3D Packing problem: Pack regular tetrahedrons into polyhedra (C++).

You must write a C++ solver that can handle ANY container size from trivial to ludicrous scale.

**Input format (stdin):**
Line 1: V edge_length
Lines 2..V+1: x y z (container vertices)

**Output format (stdout):**
Line 1: K (number of tetrahedrons placed)
Lines 2..K+1: cx cy cz rx ry rz (center + Euler angles in radians)

**Critical Requirements:**
1. **Scalability**: Your algorithm must adapt based on container volume and complexity
2. **Performance**: Must complete within 30 seconds even for massive containers
3. **Quality**: Maximize number of tetrahedrons while ensuring no overlaps

**Key Geometry (edge length e=1.0):**
- Height: h = e * sqrt(2/3) ≈ 0.8165
- Circumradius: R = e * sqrt(3/8) ≈ 0.6124
- Inradius: r = e / sqrt(24) ≈ 0.2041

**Environment:**
Platform: Windows 11 (build 10.0.26200)
Architecture: AMD64
CPU: AMD64 Family 23 Model 8 Stepping 2, AuthenticAMD

The AI must handle 3D collision detection and geometry. Subpasses use increasingly complex container shapes. The baseline uses a simple grid placement strategy.

Typical Prompt:
You are solving a Hamiltonian Path problem on 2D grids with obstacles in Rust.

You must write a Rust solver that can handle ANY grid size from trivial to ludicrous scale.

**Input format (stdin):**
Line 1: width height obstacle_count require_cycle(0/1)
Next obstacle_count lines: x y (blocked cells)

**Output format (stdout):**
Either:
- Line 1: 0 (no path exists)
OR
- Line 1: L (path length)
- Next L lines: x y coordinates of the path in order

**Critical Requirements:**
1. **Scalability**: Your algorithm must adapt based on grid size and obstacle density
2. **Performance**: Must complete within 5 minutes even for 2500 cell grids
3. **Correctness**: Must find valid paths or correctly report impossibility

**Constraints:**
- Path must start at (0, 0)
- Path must visit every non-obstacle cell exactly once
- Movement is only to adjacent cells (up/down/left/right)
- If require_cycle is True, last cell must be adjacent to (0, 0)

**Environment:**
Platform: Windows 11 (build 10.0.26200)
Architecture: AMD64
CPU: AMD64 Family 23 Model 8 Stepping 2, AuthenticAMD
Core count: 64
Memory: 127.87 GB

This is an NP-complete problem, so brute-force search blows up quickly. Subpasses increase the grid size and obstacle count. The baseline uses a simple depth-first search.

Test 6: Orbital TSP - Space Station Route Planning (C#) Score: 0.00 / 11
Typical Prompt:
You are solving an Orbital Travelling Salesman Problem in C#.

You must write a C# solver that can handle ANY number of stations from trivial to ludicrous scale:
- **Trivial**: 2-5 stations (simple brute-force permutations feasible)
- **Medium**: 6-10 stations (requires heuristics, some optimization)
- **Large**: 15-20 stations (complex orbital mechanics, needs efficient algorithms)
- **Extreme**: 50-100 stations (very complex, requires advanced heuristics)

**The Challenge:**
Your program will be tested with 2 to 100 stations. The same algorithm must work efficiently across ALL scales.

**Input format (stdin):**
Line 1: N mu
Line 2: start orbit: x y z vx vy vz
Lines 3..N+2: station orbits: x y z vx vy vz

**Output format (stdout):**
One line containing N integers: the visitation order of station indices (0..N-1).


**Starting position (epoch T=0):**
Position: (7197.9, 3733.4, -6152.3) km
Velocity: (4.567, -4.577, -1.601) km/s
Altitude: ~3807 km

**Physics constants:**
- Earth gravitational parameter μ = 398600.4418 km³/s²
- Earth radius = 6371 km

**Critical Requirements:**
1. **Scalability**: Your algorithm must adapt based on number of stations
2. **Performance**: Must complete within 5 minutes even for 100 stations
3. **Accuracy**: Should provide reasonable delta-V estimates

**Environment:**
Platform: Windows 11 (build 10.0.26200)
Architecture: AMD64
CPU: AMD64 Family 23 Model 8 Stepping 2, AuthenticAMD
Core count: 64
Memory: 127.87 GB
SIMD: AVX, AVX2, SSE, SSE2, SSE3, SSE4.1, SSE4.2, SSSE3

Visit a series of space stations orbiting Earth using the least fuel possible, then return to the starting station. Unlike a normal route-planning problem, every target is moving at thousands of metres per second and the “cost” of travelling between two stations depends on orbital physics, not straight-line distance.

The AI must combine route optimisation with orbital mechanics. Subpasses add more stations and trickier orbit geometries. The baseline uses a simple nearest-neighbour heuristic with approximate fuel estimates.

 0.0000
[2 stations] Compilation failed:
C:\Users\Ash\AppData\Local\Temp\tmpn1vl79qb.cs(6,44): error CS0246: The type or namespace name 'OrbitalNode' could not be found (are you missing a using directive or an assembly re

Orbital TSP - 2 stations

View Code (5189 chars)
namespace OrbitalTSP.Core
{
    public class OrbitalTspSolver
    {
        // Step 4.2: Implement Orbital Cost Function (Placeholder: Euclidean distance)
        public double CalculateOrbitalCost(OrbitalNode from, OrbitalNode to)
        {
            // In a real "orbital" scenario, this would involve complex astrodynamics
            // calculations (e.g., delta-v, travel time considering gravity, etc.).
            // For now, we use simple Euclidean distance as a placeholder.
            double dx = from.X - to.X;
            double dy = from.Y - to.Y;
            double dz = from.Z - to.Z;
            return Math.Sqrt(dx * dx + dy * dy + dz * dz);
        }

        // Step 4.3: Construct Cost Matrix
        public double[,] ConstructCostMatrix(List<OrbitalNode> nodes)
        {
            int n = nodes.Count;
            double[,] costMatrix = new double[n, n];

            for (int i = 0; i < n; i++)
            {
                for (int j = 0; j < n; j++)
                {
                    if (i == j)
                    {
                        costMatrix[i, j] = 0; // Cost to travel to self is 0
                    }
                    else
                    {
                        costMatrix[i, j] = CalculateOrbitalCost(nodes[i], nodes[j]);
                    }
                }
            }
            return costMatrix;
        }

        // Step 4.4: Implement TSP Algorithm (Held-Karp Dynamic Programming)
        public (List<OrbitalNode> path, double totalCost) Solve(List<OrbitalNode> nodes, OrbitalNode startNode)
        {
            int n = nodes.Count;
            if (n == 0) return (new List<OrbitalNode>(), 0);
            if (n == 1) return (new List<OrbitalNode> { nodes[0] }, 0);

            int startIndex = nodes.IndexOf(startNode);
            if (startIndex == -1)
            {
                throw new ArgumentException("Start node not found in the provided list of nodes.");
            }

            double[,] costMatrix = ConstructCostMatrix(nodes);

            // dp[mask][i] will store the minimum cost to visit all nodes in 'mask', ending at node 'i'
            double[,] dp = new double[1 << n, n];
            int[,] parent = new int[1 << n, n]; // To reconstruct the path

            // Initialize dp table with infinity
            for (int i = 0; i < (1 << n); i++)
            {
                for (int j = 0; j < n; j++)
                {
                    dp[i, j] = double.PositiveInfinity;
                }
            }

            // Base case: Starting from the startNode, visiting only one node (itself)
            dp[1 << startIndex, startIndex] = 0;

            // Iterate over all possible masks (subsets of nodes)
            for (int mask = 1; mask < (1 << n); mask++)
            {
                for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) // Current end node 'i'
                {
                    // If node 'i' is part of the current mask
                    if ((mask & (1 << i)) != 0)
                    {
                        for (int j = 0; j < n; j++) // Previous node 'j'
                        {
                            if (i == j || (mask & (1 << j)) == 0) continue; // Skip if i==j or j is not in mask

                            // If we can reach mask without 'i', ending at 'j', and then go to 'i'
                            if (dp[mask ^ (1 << i), j] + costMatrix[j, i] < dp[mask, i])
                            {
                                dp[mask, i] = dp[mask ^ (1 << i), j] + costMatrix[j, i];
                                parent[mask, i] = j;
                            }
                        }
                    }
                }
            }

            // Find the minimum cost to return to the startNode from any other node
            double minCost = double.PositiveInfinity;
            int lastNodeIndex = -1;
            int finalMask = (1 << n) - 1; // All nodes visited

            for (int i = 0; i < n; i++)
            {
                if (i == startIndex) continue;
                if (dp[finalMask, i] + costMatrix[i, startIndex] < minCost)
                {
                    minCost = dp[finalMask, i] + costMatrix[i, startIndex];
                    lastNodeIndex = i;
                }
            }

            // Path Reconstruction
            List<OrbitalNode> path = new List<OrbitalNode>();
            if (lastNodeIndex != -1)
            {
                int currentMask = finalMask;
                int currentNodeIndex = lastNodeIndex;

                while (currentNodeIndex != startIndex || currentMask != (1 << startIndex))
                {
                    path.Insert(0, nodes[currentNodeIndex]);
                    int prevNodeIndex = parent[currentMask, currentNodeIndex];
                    currentMask ^= (1 << currentNodeIndex); // Remove current node from mask
                    currentNodeIndex = prevNodeIndex;
                }
                path.Insert(0, nodes[startIndex]); // Add the start node at the beginning
                path.Add(nodes[startIndex]); // Complete the cycle by returning to the start
            }

            return (path, minCost);
        }
    }
}

Test 7: CSG Union of Two Polyhedra (C++) Score: 0.00 / 12
Typical Prompt:
You are implementing a CSG (Constructive Solid Geometry) Union operation in C++.

You must write a C++ solver that can handle ANY mesh complexity from trivial (6 vertex unit cubes) to
ludicrous scale (millions of vertices). The same program must work efficiently across ALL scales.

**Input format (stdin):**
Line 1: VA FA VB FB (vertex/face counts for mesh A and B)
Next VA lines: x y z (mesh A vertices)
Next FA lines: a b c (mesh A faces, 0-indexed)
Next VB lines: x y z (mesh B vertices)
Next FB lines: a b c (mesh B faces)

You can assume that the input geometry is clean, manifold, watertight, and free of self-intersections.

**Output format (stdout):**
Line 1: V F (vertex and face counts for union mesh)
Next V lines: x y z
Next F lines: a b c (0-indexed triangle indices)

**Critical Requirements:**
1. **Scalability**: Your algorithm must adapt based on mesh complexity
2. **Performance**: Must complete within 5 minutes even for very complex meshes
3. **Correctness**: Must output correct union geometry. Must be watertight, connected,
manifold, free of holes and self intersections..

**Environment:**
Platform: Windows 11 (build 10.0.26200)
Architecture: AMD64
CPU: AMD64 Family 23 Model 8 Stepping 2, AuthenticAMD
Core count: 64
Memory: 127.87 GB
SIMD: AVX, AVX2, SSE, SSE2, SSE3, SSE4.1, SSE4.2, SSSE3
SIMD detail: AVX=yes, AVX-512F=no, AVX2=yes, SSE=yes, SSE2=yes, SSE3=yes, SSE4.1=yes, SSE4.2=yes, SSSE3=yes

MSVC cl 19.50.35730 | std=c++20 (/std:c++20)

Be sure that any deviation from the C++ standard library is supported by the given compiler,
as referencing the wrong intrinsics or non-standard header like 'bits/stdc++.h' could fail your submission.

Given two overlapping 3D shapes (triangle meshes), merge them into a single combined shape — like gluing two lumps of clay together and keeping only the outer surface. The output mesh must be watertight with no self-intersections.

The AI must correctly handle the tricky geometry where the two shapes overlap: splitting triangles, discarding interior faces, and stitching everything back together. Subpasses use increasingly complex shape pairs.

 0.0000
[overlapping_boxes] Invalid mesh: Need at least 4 vertices for a valid polyhedron

CSG Union - overlapping_boxes

View Code (19009 chars)
#include <iostream>
#include <vector>
#include <string>
#include <sstream>
#include <algorithm>
#include <limits> // For numeric_limits
#include <cmath>  // For std::sqrt, std::abs
#include <memory> // For std::unique_ptr
#include <set>    // For std::set to count distinct intersections
#include <map>    // For std::map to manage unique vertices

// Define a small epsilon for floating-point comparisons
const double EPSILON = 1e-9;

// --- Data Structures ---

struct Vec3 {
    double x, y, z;

    // Default constructor
    Vec3() : x(0.0), y(0.0), z(0.0) {}

    // Parameterized constructor
    Vec3(double x_in, double y_in, double z_in) : x(x_in), y(y_in), z(z_in) {}

    // Vector operations
    Vec3 operator+(const Vec3& other) const { return Vec3(x + other.x, y + other.y, z + other.z); }
    Vec3 operator-(const Vec3& other) const { return Vec3(x - other.x, y - other.y, z - other.z); }
    Vec3 operator*(double scalar) const { return Vec3(x * scalar, y * scalar, z * scalar); }
    Vec3 operator/(double scalar) const { return Vec3(x / scalar, y / scalar, z / scalar); }
    double dot(const Vec3& other) const { return x * other.x + y * other.y + z * other.z; }
    Vec3 cross(const Vec3& other) const {
        return Vec3(y * other.z - z * other.y,
                    z * other.x - x * other.z,
                    x * other.y - y * other.x);
    }
    double length_sq() const { return x*x + y*y + z*z; }
    double length() const { return std::sqrt(length_sq()); }
    Vec3 normalize() const {
        double len = length();
        if (len > EPSILON) {
            return Vec3(x / len, y / len, z / len);
        }
        return Vec3(0.0, 0.0, 0.0); // Handle zero-length vector
    }

    // Comparison for sorting/hashing (consider EPSILON for floating point comparisons if needed)
    bool operator==(const Vec3& other) const {
        return std::abs(x - other.x) < EPSILON &&
               std::abs(y - other.y) < EPSILON &&
               std::abs(z - other.z) < EPSILON;
    }
    // This operator< is used for sorting in AABB tree and other places.
    // For std::map, a custom comparator `Vec3MapCompare` is used for precision control.
    bool operator<(const Vec3& other) const {
        if (x < other.x - EPSILON) return true;
        if (x > other.x + EPSILON) return false;
        if (y < other.y - EPSILON) return true;
        if (y > other.y + EPSILON) return false;
        return z < other.z - EPSILON;
    }
};

// Custom comparator for Vec3 in map to handle floating point precision
// This ensures a strict weak ordering required by std::map
struct Vec3MapCompare {
    bool operator()(const Vec3& a, const Vec3& b) const {
        if (std::abs(a.x - b.x) > EPSILON) return a.x < b.x;
        if (std::abs(a.y - b.y) > EPSILON) return a.y < b.y;
        return a.z < b.z - EPSILON; // Account for epsilon in the last dimension
    }
};


struct Triangle {
    int v[3]; // Indices into the vertex array

    Triangle(int v0, int v1, int v2) {
        v[0] = v0;
        v[1] = v1;
        v[2] = v2;
    }
};

// --- Bounding Box and AABB Tree Structures ---

struct BBox {
    Vec3 min_p = Vec3(std::numeric_limits<double>::max(), std::numeric_limits<double>::max(), std::numeric_limits<double>::max());
    Vec3 max_p = Vec3(std::numeric_limits<double>::lowest(), std::numeric_limits<double>::lowest(), std::numeric_limits<double>::lowest());

    BBox() = default;

    BBox(const Vec3& p) : min_p(p), max_p(p) {}

    BBox(const Vec3& p_min, const Vec3& p_max) : min_p(p_min), max_p(p_max) {}

    void extend(const Vec3& p) {
        min_p.x = std::min(min_p.x, p.x);
        min_p.y = std::min(min_p.y, p.y);
        min_p.z = std::min(min_p.z, p.z);
        max_p.x = std::max(max_p.x, p.x);
        max_p.y = std::max(max_p.y, p.y);
        max_p.z = std::max(max_p.z, p.z);
    }

    void extend(const BBox& other) {
        extend(other.min_p);
        extend(other.max_p);
    }
    
    // Check for ray-bbox intersection (optimized version)
    bool ray_intersects_bbox(const Vec3& ray_origin, const Vec3& ray_direction, double& t_min, double& t_max) const {
        t_min = std::numeric_limits<double>::lowest();
        t_max = std::numeric_limits<double>::max();

        for (int i = 0; i < 3; ++i) {
            double inv_d = 1.0 / (&ray_direction.x)[i];
            double t0 = (((&min_p.x)[i] - (&ray_origin.x)[i]) * inv_d);
            double t1 = (((&max_p.x)[i] - (&ray_origin.x)[i]) * inv_d);

            if (inv_d < 0.0) std::swap(t0, t1);

            t_min = std::max(t0, t_min);
            t_max = std::min(t1, t_max);

            if (t_max <= t_min) return false;
        }
        return true;
    }
};

struct AABBTreeNode {
    BBox bbox;
    std::vector<int> triangle_indices; // Indices to triangles in the Mesh's faces vector
    std::unique_ptr<AABBTreeNode> left;
    std::unique_ptr<AABBTreeNode> right;

    bool is_leaf() const {
        return !left && !right;
    }
};

struct Mesh {
    std::vector<Vec3> vertices;
    std::vector<Triangle> faces;
    std::unique_ptr<AABBTreeNode> aabb_tree_root; // Root of the AABB tree
};

// --- Helper Functions for Input/Output ---

void read_mesh(Mesh& mesh) {
    int v_count, f_count;
    std::cin >> v_count >> f_count;

    mesh.vertices.reserve(v_count);
    for (int i = 0; i < v_count; ++i) {
        double x, y, z;
        std::cin >> x >> y >> z;
        mesh.vertices.emplace_back(x, y, z);
    }

    mesh.faces.reserve(f_count);
    for (int i = 0; i < f_count; ++i) {
        int v0, v1, v2;
        std::cin >> v0 >> v1 >> v2;
        mesh.faces.emplace_back(v0, v1, v2);
    }
}

void write_mesh(const Mesh& mesh) {
    std::cout << mesh.vertices.size() << " " << mesh.faces.size() << std::endl;
    std::cout.precision(std::numeric_limits<double>::max_digits10); // Ensure full double precision for output
    for (const auto& v : mesh.vertices) {
        std::cout << v.x << " " << v.y << " " << v.z << std::endl;
    }
    for (const auto& f : mesh.faces) {
        std::cout << f.v[0] << " " << f.v[1] << " " << f.v[2] << std::endl;
    }
}

// --- Geometric Helper Functions ---

// Calculate the centroid of a triangle
Vec3 get_triangle_centroid(const Vec3& v0, const Vec3& v1, const Vec3& v2) {
    return (v0 + v1 + v2) / 3.0;
}

// Calculate the normal of a triangle
Vec3 get_triangle_normal(const Vec3& v0, const Vec3& v1, const Vec3& v2) {
    Vec3 edge1 = v1 - v0;
    Vec3 edge2 = v2 - v0;
    return edge1.cross(edge2).normalize();
}

// Moller-Trumbore algorithm for ray-triangle intersection
// Returns true if ray intersects triangle, and sets t_out to the distance along the ray
bool ray_intersects_triangle(const Vec3& ray_origin, const Vec3& ray_direction,
                             const Vec3& v0, const Vec3& v1, const Vec3& v2,
                             double& t_out) {
    Vec3 edge1 = v1 - v0;
    Vec3 edge2 = v2 - v0;
    Vec3 pvec = ray_direction.cross(edge2);
    double det = edge1.dot(pvec);

    // If det is close to 0, ray is parallel to triangle plane
    if (std::abs(det) < EPSILON) return false;

    double inv_det = 1.0 / det;

    Vec3 tvec = ray_origin - v0;
    double u = tvec.dot(pvec) * inv_det;
    if (u < 0.0 - EPSILON || u > 1.0 + EPSILON) return false;

    Vec3 qvec = tvec.cross(edge1);
    double v = ray_direction.dot(qvec) * inv_det;
    if (v < 0.0 - EPSILON || u + v > 1.0 + EPSILON) return false;

    t_out = edge2.dot(qvec) * inv_det;

    // Only consider intersections in the positive ray direction (t > 0)
    // And also discard intersections too close to the origin to avoid self-intersection issues
    return t_out > EPSILON;
}


// --- AABB Tree Construction and Traversal ---

// Recursive function to build the AABB tree
std::unique_ptr<AABBTreeNode> build_aabb_tree_recursive(const Mesh& mesh, std::vector<int>& triangle_indices, int depth = 0) {
    if (triangle_indices.empty()) {
        return nullptr;
    }

    std::unique_ptr<AABBTreeNode> node = std::make_unique<AABBTreeNode>();

    // Compute bounding box for current set of triangles
    for (int tri_idx : triangle_indices) {
        const Triangle& tri = mesh.faces[tri_idx];
        node->bbox.extend(mesh.vertices[tri.v[0]]);
        node->bbox.extend(mesh.vertices[tri.v[1]]);
        node->bbox.extend(mesh.vertices[tri.v[2]]);
    }

    // If too few triangles or max depth reached, make it a leaf node
    if (triangle_indices.size() <= 4 || depth > 20) { // Max 4 triangles per leaf, max depth 20
        node->triangle_indices = std::move(triangle_indices);
        return node;
    }

    // Determine the longest axis of the bounding box
    Vec3 diag = node->bbox.max_p - node->bbox.min_p;
    int axis = 0;
    if (diag.y > diag.x) axis = 1;
    if (diag.z > diag.y && diag.z > diag.x) axis = 2;

    // Sort triangles by centroid along the chosen axis
    std::sort(triangle_indices.begin(), triangle_indices.end(), [&](int a, int b) {
        Vec3 centroid_a = get_triangle_centroid(mesh.vertices[mesh.faces[a].v[0]],
                                                mesh.vertices[mesh.faces[a].v[1]],
                                                mesh.vertices[mesh.faces[a].v[2]]);
        Vec3 centroid_b = get_triangle_centroid(mesh.vertices[mesh.faces[b].v[0]],
                                                mesh.vertices[mesh.faces[b].v[1]],
                                                mesh.vertices[mesh.faces[b].v[2]]);
        return ((&centroid_a.x)[axis] < (&centroid_b.x)[axis]);
    });

    // Split into two halves
    int mid = triangle_indices.size() / 2;
    std::vector<int> left_indices(triangle_indices.begin(), triangle_indices.begin() + mid);
    std::vector<int> right_indices(triangle_indices.begin() + mid, triangle_indices.end());

    node->left = build_aabb_tree_recursive(mesh, left_indices, depth + 1);
    node->right = build_aabb_tree_recursive(mesh, right_indices, depth + 1);

    return node;
}

void build_aabb_tree(Mesh& mesh) {
    if (mesh.faces.empty()) {
        mesh.aabb_tree_root = nullptr;
        return;
    }

    std::vector<int> all_triangle_indices(mesh.faces.size());
    for (int i = 0; i < mesh.faces.size(); ++i) {
        all_triangle_indices[i] = i;
    }
    mesh.aabb_tree_root = build_aabb_tree_recursive(mesh, all_triangle_indices);
}


// Determine if a point is inside a mesh using ray casting and AABB tree
// Assumes a watertight, manifold mesh.
// Casts a ray in a random direction and counts intersections.
// Odd number of intersections means inside, even means outside.
bool is_point_inside_mesh(const Vec3& point, const Mesh& mesh) {
    if (mesh.faces.empty() || !mesh.aabb_tree_root) return false; // Cannot determine for an empty mesh or missing tree

    // Choose a random direction (fixed for simplicity, robustness requires truly random or multiple directions)
    // A direction that is unlikely to be parallel to any face normals of a typical mesh.
    Vec3 ray_direction(0.70710678, 0.70710678, 0.0); // Example: (1/sqrt(2), 1/sqrt(2), 0) normalized

    std::set<double> intersection_ts_set; // Use a set to automatically handle unique and sorted intersections

    std::vector<AABBTreeNode*> nodes_to_visit;
    nodes_to_visit.push_back(mesh.aabb_tree_root.get());

    while (!nodes_to_visit.empty()) {
        AABBTreeNode* current_node = nodes_to_visit.back();
        nodes_to_visit.pop_back();

        double t_min_bbox, t_max_bbox;
        // Optimization: if point is inside bbox, no need to check ray_intersects_bbox
        // But for a true ray, we need to check if the ray from point intersects the bbox
        if (!current_node->bbox.ray_intersects_bbox(point, ray_direction, t_min_bbox, t_max_bbox)) {
            continue;
        }

        if (current_node->is_leaf()) {
            for (int tri_idx : current_node->triangle_indices) {
                const Triangle& tri = mesh.faces[tri_idx];
                const Vec3& v0 = mesh.vertices[tri.v[0]];
                const Vec3& v1 = mesh.vertices[tri.v[1]];
                const Vec3& v2 = mesh.vertices[tri.v[2]];

                double t_hit;
                if (ray_intersects_triangle(point, ray_direction, v0, v1, v2, t_hit)) {
                    intersection_ts_set.insert(t_hit);
                }
            }
        } else {
            // Traverse children
            if (current_node->left) {
                nodes_to_visit.push_back(current_node->left.get());
            }
            if (current_node->right) {
                nodes_to_visit.push_back(current_node->right.get());
            }
        }
    }
    
    // The number of intersections is the size of the set.
    return (intersection_ts_set.size() % 2) == 1;
}

// --- Main CSG Union Logic ---
Mesh csg_union(const Mesh& mesh_a, const Mesh& mesh_b) {
    Mesh union_mesh;
    // For this simplified approach, we are not handling intersections explicitly.
    // This will produce an "approximate" union where:
    // - Triangles from A that are outside B are kept.
    // - Triangles from B that are outside A are kept.
    // Intersecting regions are not correctly handled, and new faces are not generated.

    // To manage unique vertices and mapping
    std::vector<Vec3> temp_vertices;
    std::vector<Triangle> temp_faces;
    
    // Using custom comparator for Vec3 to handle floating point precision in map keys
    std::map<Vec3, int, Vec3MapCompare> vertex_to_index_map;

    auto add_face_to_union = [&](const Vec3& v0, const Vec3& v1, const Vec3& v2) {
        auto get_or_add_vertex = [&](const Vec3& p) {
            auto it = vertex_to_index_map.find(p);
            if (it == vertex_to_index_map.end()) {
                int new_index = temp_vertices.size();
                temp_vertices.push_back(p);
                vertex_to_index_map[p] = new_index;
                return new_index;
            }
            return it->second;
        };

        int idx0 = get_or_add_vertex(v0);
        int idx1 = get_or_add_vertex(v1);
        int idx2 = get_or_add_vertex(v2);
        temp_faces.emplace_back(idx0, idx1, idx2);
    };


    // Stage 1: Classify and add non-intersecting triangles
    // Add faces from mesh_a that are entirely OUTSIDE mesh_b
    // This classification is based on the triangle's centroid.
    // LIMITATION: This approach is an approximation. Triangles that straddle the boundary
    // (partially inside and partially outside) will be incorrectly classified if their
    // centroid falls on the "wrong" side. This can lead to holes or incorrect geometry
    // where meshes overlap. A robust solution requires precise clipping
    // of intersecting triangles.
    for (const auto& face : mesh_a.faces) {
        const Vec3& v0 = mesh_a.vertices[face.v[0]];
        const Vec3& v1 = mesh_a.vertices[face.v[1]];
        const Vec3& v2 = mesh_a.vertices[face.v[2]];
        Vec3 centroid = get_triangle_centroid(v0, v1, v2);

        if (!is_point_inside_mesh(centroid, mesh_b)) {
            add_face_to_union(v0, v1, v2);
        }
    }

    // Add faces from mesh_b whose centroids are outside mesh_a
    // Same limitation as above applies.
    for (const auto& face : mesh_b.faces) {
        const Vec3& v0 = mesh_b.vertices[face.v[0]];
        const Vec3& v1 = mesh_b.vertices[face.v[1]];
        const Vec3& v2 = mesh_b.vertices[face.v[2]];
        Vec3 centroid = get_triangle_centroid(v0, v1, v2);

        if (!is_point_inside_mesh(centroid, mesh_a)) {
            add_face_to_union(v0, v1, v2);
        }
    }

    // Stage 2: Handling Intersecting Regions (Critical Missing Component for Robust CSG)
    //
    // A robust Constructive Solid Geometry (CSG) Union algorithm, especially for
    // "clean, manifold, watertight, and free of self-intersections" inputs
    // and demanding "correctness" with "ludicrous scale" meshes, would require:
    //
    // 1.  **Exact Triangle-Triangle Intersection Detection & Computation:**
    //     Efficiently find all pairs of triangles (one from mesh_a, one from mesh_b)
    //     that intersect. For each intersecting pair, compute the exact 3D line segment
    //     of intersection. AABB trees are crucial for pruning non-intersecting pairs.
    //
    // 2.  **Triangle Splitting/Clipping:**
    //     For every triangle that is intersected by another mesh, it must be split
    //     into multiple smaller (sub-)triangles or polygons. These smaller pieces
    //     then need to be individually classified. This often involves 2D polygon
    //     clipping algorithms applied in the plane of the triangle, projected from 3D.
    //
    // 3.  **Classification of Clipped Fragments:**
    //     Each new fragment resulting from the splitting must be accurately classified
    //     as either "inside" or "outside" the *other* mesh. For a union, "inside"
    //     fragments are typically discarded. This classification might involve checking
    //     a point on the fragment (e.g., centroid) using an accelerated point-in-mesh
    //     test, but careful handling is needed for points very close to the boundary.
    //
    // 4.  **Boundary Reconstruction (New Faces):**
    //     The intersection line segments computed in step 1 form a closed loop (or loops)
    //     on the surface where the two meshes cut each other. These loops must be
    //     triangulated to form new faces that connect the "outside" parts of the two
    //     original meshes, ensuring the resulting union mesh is watertight.
    //
    // 5.  **Topological Operations & Cleaning:**
    //     After collecting all fragments and new boundary faces, a topological merge
    //     is necessary. This involves:
    //     - Merging coincident vertices (as attempted by `vertex_to_index_map` above)
    //       to ensure a truly manifold output and prevent T-junctions or cracks.
    //     - Removing degenerate triangles (zero area).
    //     - Re-indexing all vertices and faces to form a coherent new mesh.
    //
    // This current implementation *does not perform* these steps for intersecting regions.
    // It relies solely on centroid-based classification, which is a heuristic and will
    // produce an incorrect or non-watertight result for meshes that actually intersect
    // each other beyond simple containment.
    
    union_mesh.vertices = std::move(temp_vertices);
    union_mesh.faces = std::move(temp_faces);

    return union_mesh;
}


// --- Main Function ---

int main() {
    // Optimize C++ streams for faster input/output
    std::ios_base::sync_with_stdio(false);
    std::cin.tie(NULL);
    std::cout.tie(NULL);

    Mesh mesh_a, mesh_b;

    // Read Mesh A
    read_mesh(mesh_a);
    build_aabb_tree(mesh_a); // Build AABB tree for mesh A

    // Read Mesh B
    read_mesh(mesh_b);
    build_aabb_tree(mesh_b); // Build AABB tree for mesh B

    // Perform CSG Union operation
    Mesh union_mesh = csg_union(mesh_a, mesh_b);

    // Write the resulting union mesh
    write_mesh(union_mesh);

    return 0;
}

Test 8: Maze Solver (Rust) Score: 0.00 / 14
Typical Prompt:
You are writing a maze solver in Rust that must handle ANY maze complexity from trivial to ludicrous scale.

Your program will be tested with mazes ranging from 5x5 to 50000x50000.
The same program must work efficiently across ALL scales.

**Maze format:**
- 'A' = Start position
- 'B' = Goal/End position
- '#' = Wall (cannot pass through)
- ' ' (space) = Open path (can walk through)

**Input format (stdin):**
Line 1: H W (height and width)
Next H lines: the maze row strings (each W characters)

**Output format (stdout):**
Line 1: N (number of steps in path)
Next N lines: x y (0-indexed coordinates from top-left)
Path should include both start (A) and end (B) positions.
Movement is only up/down/left/right (no diagonals).
Output nothing (or 0) if no path exists.

**Constraints:**
- Path must be continuous (each step adjacent to previous)
- Path must not go through walls ('#')
- Path must start at 'A' and end at 'B'
- Path must not loop even if the maze allows it.
- If multiple solutions exist, return any one.

**Environment:**
Platform: Windows 11 (build 10.0.26200)
Architecture: AMD64
CPU: AMD64 Family 23 Model 8 Stepping 2, AuthenticAMD
Core count: 64
Memory: 127.87 GB
SIMD: AVX, AVX2, SSE, SSE2, SSE3, SSE4.1, SSE4.2, SSSE3
SIMD detail: AVX=yes, AVX-512F=no, AVX2=yes, SSE=yes, SSE2=yes, SSE3=yes, SSE4.1=yes, SSE4.2=yes, SSSE3=yes

**Rust Compiler:**
rustc 1.92.0 (ded5c06cf 2025-12-08) | edition=2024 | host: x86_64-pc-windows-msvc

Write complete, compilable Rust code with a main() function.
 Prompt Changes:
Prompt parameters change between subpasses (increasing difficulty)

Find the shortest path through a maze from a start cell to an end cell. The maze is a grid of walls and open passages, and the AI must navigate from one corner to the other using the fewest steps possible.

Subpasses increase the maze size dramatically, testing whether the AI's approach scales to very large mazes. The baseline finds the true shortest path using breadth-first search.

Maze Solver - 5x5

Algorithm: !/bin/sh Copyright (c) 2006, 2008 Junio C Hamano The "pre-rebase" hook is run just before "git rebase" starts doing its job, and can prevent the command from running by exiting with non-zero status. The hook is called with the following parameters: $1 -- the upstream the series was forked from. $2 -- the branch being rebased (or empty when rebasing the current branch). This sample shows how to prevent topic branches that are already merged to 'next' branch from getting rebased, because allo...

View Code (4310 chars)
publish=next
basebranch="$1"
if test "$#" = 2
then
	topic="refs/heads/$2"
else
	topic=`git symbolic-ref HEAD` ||
	exit 0 ;# we do not interrupt rebasing detached HEAD
fi

case "$topic" in
refs/heads/??/*)
	;;
*)
	exit 0 ;# we do not interrupt others.
	;;
esac

# Now we are dealing with a topic branch being rebased
# on top of master.  Is it OK to rebase it?

# Does the topic really exist?
git show-ref -q "$topic" || {
	echo >&2 "No such branch $topic"
	exit 1
}

# Is topic fully merged to master?
not_in_master=`git rev-list --pretty=oneline ^master "$topic"`
if test -z "$not_in_master"
then
	echo >&2 "$topic is fully merged to master; better remove it."
	exit 1 ;# we could allow it, but there is no point.
fi

# Is topic ever merged to next?  If so you should not be rebasing it.
only_next_1=`git rev-list ^master "^$topic" ${publish} | sort`
only_next_2=`git rev-list ^master           ${publish} | sort`
if test "$only_next_1" = "$only_next_2"
then
	not_in_topic=`git rev-list "^$topic" master`
	if test -z "$not_in_topic"
	then
		echo >&2 "$topic is already up to date with master"
		exit 1 ;# we could allow it, but there is no point.
	else
		exit 0
	fi
else
	not_in_next=`git rev-list --pretty=oneline ^${publish} "$topic"`
	/usr/bin/perl -e '
		my $topic = $ARGV[0];
		my $msg = "* $topic has commits already merged to public branch:\n";
		my (%not_in_next) = map {
			/^([0-9a-f]+) /;
			($1 => 1);
		} split(/\n/, $ARGV[1]);
		for my $elem (map {
				/^([0-9a-f]+) (.*)$/;
				[$1 => $2];
			} split(/\n/, $ARGV[2])) {
			if (!exists $not_in_next{$elem->[0]}) {
				if ($msg) {
					print STDERR $msg;
					undef $msg;
				}
				print STDERR " $elem->[1]\n";
			}
		}
	' "$topic" "$not_in_next" "$not_in_master"
	exit 1
fi

<<\DOC_END

This sample hook safeguards topic branches that have been
published from being rewound.

The workflow assumed here is:

 * Once a topic branch forks from "master", "master" is never
   merged into it again (either directly or indirectly).

 * Once a topic branch is fully cooked and merged into "master",
   it is deleted.  If you need to build on top of it to correct
   earlier mistakes, a new topic branch is created by forking at
   the tip of the "master".  This is not strictly necessary, but
   it makes it easier to keep your history simple.

 * Whenever you need to test or publish your changes to topic
   branches, merge them into "next" branch.

The script, being an example, hardcodes the publish branch name
to be "next", but it is trivial to make it configurable via
$GIT_DIR/config mechanism.

With this workflow, you would want to know:

(1) ... if a topic branch has ever been merged to "next".  Young
    topic branches can have stupid mistakes you would rather
    clean up before publishing, and things that have not been
    merged into other branches can be easily rebased without
    affecting other people.  But once it is published, you would
    not want to rewind it.

(2) ... if a topic branch has been fully merged to "master".
    Then you can delete it.  More importantly, you should not
    build on top of it -- other people may already want to
    change things related to the topic as patches against your
    "master", so if you need further changes, it is better to
    fork the topic (perhaps with the same name) afresh from the
    tip of "master".

Let's look at this example:

		   o---o---o---o---o---o---o---o---o---o "next"
		  /       /           /           /
		 /   a---a---b A     /           /
		/   /               /           /
	       /   /   c---c---c---c B         /
	      /   /   /             \         /
	     /   /   /   b---b C     \       /
	    /   /   /   /             \     /
    ---o---o---o---o---o---o---o---o---o---o---o "master"


A, B and C are topic branches.

 * A has one fix since it was merged up to "next".

 * B has finished.  It has been fully merged up to "master" and "next",
   and is ready to be deleted.

 * C has not merged to "next" at all.

We would want to allow C to be rebased, refuse A, and encourage
B to be deleted.

To compute (1):

	git rev-list ^master ^topic next
	git rev-list ^master        next

	if these match, topic has not merged in next at all.

To compute (2):

	git rev-list master..topic

	if this is empty, it is fully merged to "master".

DOC_END

Test 9: 1D Cutting Stock - Minimum Waste (C#) Score: 10.50 / 15
Typical Prompt:
You are solving the 1D Cutting Stock Problem in C#.

**Input format (stdin):**
Line 1: N stock_length
Line 2: N cut lengths separated by spaces

**Output format (stdout):**
Line 1: num_stocks
Next num_stocks lines: space-separated cut indices assigned to that stock (0-indexed)

**Example:**
For cuts=[30, 40, 50] and stock_length=100:
Input:
3 100
30 40 50

Output:
2
0 1
2

**Constraints:**
- Each cut must come from a single stock piece (no gluing)
- Multiple cuts can come from the same stock piece if they fit
- No cut can exceed stock_length
- Must handle varying numbers of cuts efficiently. You can use
multiple threads, you can micro-optimise code, you can write clever algorithms,
whatever it takes to handle the problem and solve it within 5 minutes even if
there are millions of cuts.

**Environment:**
Platform: Windows 11 (build 10.0.26200)
Architecture: AMD64
CPU: AMD64 Family 23 Model 8 Stepping 2, AuthenticAMD
Core count: 64
Memory: 127.87 GB
SIMD: AVX, AVX2, SSE, SSE2, SSE3, SSE4.1, SSE4.2, SSSE3
SIMD detail: AVX=yes, AVX-512F=no, AVX2=yes, SSE=yes, SSE2=yes, SSE3=yes, SSE4.1=yes, SSE4.2=yes, SSSE3=yes

**C# Compiler:**
C# (csc.exe 5.5.0-2.26208.13 (3dc781932f83b0792b0d1717a68db3f9604a2cb1)) | max LangVersion 14

Write complete, compilable C# code with a static void Main method.

Subpass 0
Parameters: (see typical prompt)View exact prompt
View raw AI output
View chain of thought

Given fixed-length stock material and a shopping list of smaller pieces to cut, use as few stock pieces as possible. Every requested piece must be cut from somewhere, but any leftover material is wasted — so packing the cuts tightly matters.

This is essentially a bin-packing problem and is NP-hard. Subpasses scale from a handful of cuts up to millions, forcing the AI to write code that is both correct and fast. The baseline uses a simple first-fit-decreasing heuristic.

 1.0000
[5 cuts, stock=100] Stocks used: 2, Waste: 20, Time: 0.1s - (waste < 1 stock, no repack check)

Cutting Stock - 5 cuts, stock=100

View Code (2360 chars)
using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;

public class Program
{
    public static void Main(string[] args)
    {
        // Read N and stock_length
        string[] firstLine = Console.ReadLine().Split(' ');
        int N = int.Parse(firstLine[0]);
        int stockLength = int.Parse(firstLine[1]);

        // Read cut lengths
        List<int> cutLengths = Console.ReadLine().Split(' ').Select(int.Parse).ToList();

        // Create a list of Cut objects with original indices
        List<Cut> cuts = new List<Cut>();
        for (int i = 0; i < N; i++)
        {
            cuts.Add(new Cut { Length = cutLengths[i], OriginalIndex = i });
        }

        // Sort cuts in descending order of length for First Fit Decreasing (FFD)
        cuts = cuts.OrderByDescending(c => c.Length).ToList();

        List<Stock> stocks = new List<Stock>();

        foreach (Cut cut in cuts)
        {
            bool placed = false;
            foreach (Stock stock in stocks)
            {
                if (stock.RemainingCapacity >= cut.Length)
                {
                    stock.AddCut(cut);
                    placed = true;
                    break;
                }
            }

            if (!placed)
            {
                Stock newStock = new Stock(stockLength);
                newStock.AddCut(cut);
                stocks.Add(newStock);
            }
        }

        // Output results
        Console.WriteLine(stocks.Count);
        foreach (Stock stock in stocks)
        {
            Console.WriteLine(string.Join(" ", stock.CutIndices.OrderBy(idx => idx)));
        }
    }
}

public class Cut
{
    public int Length { get; set; }
    public int OriginalIndex { get; set; }
}

public class Stock
{
    public int Capacity { get; }
    public int RemainingCapacity { get; private set; }
    public List<int> CutIndices { get; }

    public Stock(int capacity)
    {
        Capacity = capacity;
        RemainingCapacity = capacity;
        CutIndices = new List<int>();
    }

    public void AddCut(Cut cut)
    {
        if (RemainingCapacity >= cut.Length)
        {
            RemainingCapacity -= cut.Length;
            CutIndices.Add(cut.OriginalIndex);
        }
        else
        {
            throw new InvalidOperationException("Cut does not fit in stock.");
        }
    }
}
Cut Plan Visualization (5 cuts, stock=100)

Colored segments are cuts (labels show lengths when there is room). Red segment is waste.

Test 10: 2D Cutting Stock - Rectangle Packing (C++) Score: 0.00 / 13
Typical Prompt:
You are solving the 2D Cutting Stock Problem (Rectangle Bin Packing) in C++.

Given the option to purchase fixed size large 2D boards, and a list of rectangles needed,
minimize the number of boards purchased by cleverly planning your cuts and minimising waste.

You must write a C++ solver that can handle ANY problem size from trivial to ludicrous scale:
- **Trivial**: 4-8 rectangles, 100x100-200x150 boards
- **Ludicrous**: 500-50000 rectangles, 2000x2000-10000x10000 boards

**Input format (stdin):**
Line 1: N board_width board_height
Next N lines: w h (rectangle dimensions)

**Output format (stdout):**
Line 1: num_boards
Next N lines: board_index x y rotated (one per rectangle, rotated is 0 or 1)

**Critical Requirements:**
1. **Scalability**: Your algorithm must adapt based on problem size and board dimensions
2. **Performance**: Must complete within 30 seconds even for 50000 rectangles
3. **Quality**: Minimize number of boards used while producing valid placements

**Constraints:**
- Rectangles may be rotated 90° if it helps
- No overlapping allowed
- Rectangles must not exceed board boundaries

**Environment:**
Platform: Windows 11 (build 10.0.26200)
Architecture: AMD64
CPU: AMD64 Family 23 Model 8 Stepping 2, AuthenticAMD
Core count: 64
Memory: 127.87 GB
SIMD: AVX, AVX2, SSE, SSE2, SSE3, SSE4.1, SSE4.2, SSSE3
SIMD detail: AVX=yes, AVX-512F=no, AVX2=yes, SSE=yes, SSE2=yes, SSE3=yes, SSE4.1=yes, SSE4.2=yes, SSSE3=yes

as referencing the wrong intrinsics or non-standard header like 'bits/stdc++.h' could fail your submission.

View chain of thought

Pack a set of rectangles into as few fixed-size boards as possible, with no overlapping. Rectangles may be rotated 90° to fit better. Think of it like fitting as many labels as you can onto sheets of card stock.

This 2D packing problem is NP-hard. Subpasses increase the number and variety of rectangles. The baseline stacks rectangles in simple horizontal rows.

 0.0000
[4 rectangles, 100x100 board] Compilation failed:
tmpe54q0dmq.cpp
C:\Users\Ash\AppData\Local\Temp\tmpe54q0dmq.cpp(209): error C2065: 'bins': undeclared identifier
C:\Users\Ash\AppData\Local\Temp\tmpe54q0dmq.cpp(210): error C2065:

2D Cutting Stock - 4 rectangles, 100x100 board

View Code (11149 chars)
#include <iostream>
#include <vector>
#include <algorithm>
#include <tuple> // For std::tie
#include <limits> // For std::numeric_limits

// Represents a rectangle
struct Rect {
    int x, y, width, height;

    Rect(int x = 0, int y = 0, int width = 0, int height = 0)
        : x(x), y(y), width(width), height(height) {}

    int area() const { return width * height; }

    bool intersects(const Rect& other) const {
        return x < other.x + other.width &&
               x + width > other.x &&
               y < other.y + other.height &&
               y + height > other.y;
    }
     // Checks if this rectangle contains another rectangle
    bool contains(const Rect& other) const {
        return x <= other.x &&
               y <= other.y &&
               x + width >= other.x + other.width &&
               y + height >= other.y + other.height;
    }
};

// Represents an item to be packed
struct Item {
    int id;
    int originalWidth, originalHeight; // Store original dimensions
    int currentWidth, currentHeight; // Store current dimensions (might be rotated)
    int x, y;
    bool rotated;
    int binIndex; // New member to store the bin index

    Item(int id, int width, int height)
        : id(id), originalWidth(width), originalHeight(height),
          currentWidth(width), currentHeight(height),
          x(0), y(0), rotated(false), binIndex(-1) {} // Initialize binIndex to -1

    int area() const { return originalWidth * originalHeight; }

    // Helper to try rotating the item
    void rotate() {
        std::swap(currentWidth, currentHeight);
        rotated = !rotated;
    }

    // Reset to original orientation
    void resetRotation() {
        if (rotated) {
            std::swap(currentWidth, currentHeight);
            rotated = false;
        }
    }
};

// MaxRectsBinPack algorithm implementation for a single bin
class MaxRectsBinPack {
public:
    MaxRectsBinPack(int binWidth, int binHeight)
        : binWidth_(binWidth), binHeight_(binHeight) {
        freeRectangles_.push_back(Rect(0, 0, binWidth, binHeight));
    }

    // Tries to insert a rectangle into the bin using a best-fit heuristic
    // Returns the placed rectangle if successful, otherwise an empty Rect
    Rect Insert(int width, int height, bool canRotate, Item& item) {
        int bestRectIndex = -1;
        int bestScore1 = std::numeric_limits<int>::max(); // e.g., min short side fit
        int bestScore2 = std::numeric_limits<int>::max(); // e.g., min long side fit (tie-breaker)

        Rect bestRect;
        bool rotated = false;

        for (size_t i = 0; i < freeRectangles_.size(); ++i) {
            const Rect& freeRect = freeRectangles_[i];

            // Try non-rotated
            if (freeRect.width >= width && freeRect.height >= height) {
                int score1 = std::min(freeRect.width - width, freeRect.height - height); // Short side fit
                int score2 = std::max(freeRect.width - width, freeRect.height - height); // Long side fit
                
                if (score1 < bestScore1 || (score1 == bestScore1 && score2 < bestScore2)) {
                    bestScore1 = score1;
                    bestScore2 = score2;
                    bestRectIndex = i;
                    bestRect = Rect(freeRect.x, freeRect.y, width, height);
                    rotated = false;
                }
            }

            // Try rotated
            if (canRotate && width != height && freeRect.width >= height && freeRect.height >= width) {
                int score1 = std::min(freeRect.width - height, freeRect.height - width); // Short side fit
                int score2 = std::max(freeRect.width - height, freeRect.height - width); // Long side fit

                if (score1 < bestScore1 || (score1 == bestScore1 && score2 < bestScore2)) {
                    bestScore1 = score1;
                    bestScore2 = score2;
                    bestRectIndex = i;
                    bestRect = Rect(freeRect.x, freeRect.y, height, width); // Note: width and height swapped
                    rotated = true;
                }
            }
        }

        if (bestRectIndex != -1) {
            Rect freeRect = freeRectangles_[bestRectIndex];
            PlaceRect(bestRect, freeRect);
            item.x = bestRect.x;
            item.y = bestRect.y;
            item.rotated = rotated;
            if (rotated) {
                std::swap(item.currentWidth, item.currentHeight);
            }
            return bestRect;
        }

        return Rect(); // No fit found
    }

private:
    int binWidth_;
    int binHeight_;
    std::vector<Rect> freeRectangles_;

    void PlaceRect(const Rect& placedRect, Rect& freeRect) {
        // Remove the free rectangle that was used
        auto it = std::find_if(freeRectangles_.begin(), freeRectangles_.end(), 
                               [&](const Rect& r){ return r.x == freeRect.x && r.y == freeRect.y && r.width == freeRect.width && r.height == freeRect.height; });
        if (it != freeRectangles_.end()) {
            freeRectangles_.erase(it);
        }

        // Split the free rectangle into at most two new free rectangles (L-shaped split).
        // This is a more robust splitting strategy for MaxRectsBinPack.

        // Create a new free rectangle for the space above the placed rectangle
        if (freeRect.y + freeRect.height > placedRect.y + placedRect.height) {
            Rect newRect(freeRect.x, placedRect.y + placedRect.height,
                         freeRect.width, freeRect.y + freeRect.height - (placedRect.y + placedRect.height));
            freeRectangles_.push_back(newRect);
        }

        // Create a new free rectangle for the space to the right of the placed rectangle
        // This rectangle's height is limited to the placed rectangle's height.
        if (freeRect.x + freeRect.width > placedRect.x + placedRect.width) {
            Rect newRect(placedRect.x + placedRect.width, placedRect.y,
                         freeRect.x + freeRect.width - (placedRect.x + placedRect.width), placedRect.height);
            freeRectangles_.push_back(newRect);
        }
        
        PruneFreeRectangles();
    }

    void PruneFreeRectangles() {
        // Remove free rectangles that are contained within others.
        // This loop is O(N^2) but typically N is small.
        for (size_t i = 0; i < freeRectangles_.size(); ++i) {
            for (size_t j = i + 1; j < freeRectangles_.size(); ++j) {
                if (freeRectangles_[j].contains(freeRectangles_[i])) {
                    freeRectangles_.erase(freeRectangles_.begin() + i);
                    --i;
                    break; 
                }
                if (freeRectangles_[i].contains(freeRectangles_[j])) {
                    freeRectangles_.erase(freeRectangles_.begin() + j);
                    --j;
                }
            }
        }
        // After pruning, sort the free rectangles to keep them somewhat organized.
        // This might help with cache locality or certain heuristics.
        std::sort(freeRectangles_.begin(), freeRectangles_.end(), [](const Rect& a, const Rect& b) {
            if (a.y != b.y) return a.y < b.y;
            return a.x < b.x;
        });
    }
};

// Main solver class
class Solver {
public:
    Solver(int boardWidth, int boardHeight)
        : boardWidth_(boardWidth), boardHeight_(boardHeight) {}

    void solve(std::vector<Item>& items) {
        // Sort items by a heuristic (e.g., decreasing original area)
        std::sort(items.begin(), items.end(), [](const Item& a, const Item& b) {
            return a.area() > b.area();
        });



        // Map to store the final placement for each original item ID
        // This is necessary because items vector is sorted, and we need to output by original ID.
        std::vector<Item> finalPlacedItems(items.size());

        for (auto& item : items) {
            bool placed = false;
            int currentBinIndex = -1;

            // Try to place in existing bins
            for (size_t i = 0; i < bins.size(); ++i) {
                MaxRectsBinPack& currentBin = bins[i];
                Item tempItem = item; // Create a temporary item for insertion attempt
                
                Rect placedRect = currentBin.Insert(tempItem.currentWidth, tempItem.currentHeight, true, tempItem);
                
                if (placedRect.area() > 0) {
                    // Placement successful, update original item and break
                    item.x = placedRect.x;
                    item.y = placedRect.y;
                    item.rotated = tempItem.rotated;
                    item.currentWidth = tempItem.currentWidth;
                    item.currentHeight = tempItem.currentHeight;

                    item.binIndex = i; // Store the bin index
                    finalPlacedItems[item.id] = item; // Store by original ID
                    placed = true;
                    // currentBinIndex = i; // This variable is no longer strictly needed
                    break;
                }
            }

            // If not placed, create a new bin
            if (!placed) {
                bins.emplace_back(boardWidth_, boardHeight_);
                MaxRectsBinPack& newBin = bins.back();
                
                Item tempItem = item; // Create a temporary item for insertion attempt
                Rect placedRect = newBin.Insert(tempItem.currentWidth, tempItem.currentHeight, true, tempItem);

                if (placedRect.area() > 0) {
                    item.x = placedRect.x;
                    item.y = placedRect.y;
                    item.rotated = tempItem.rotated;
                    item.currentWidth = tempItem.currentWidth;
                    item.currentHeight = tempItem.currentHeight;
                    item.binIndex = bins.size() - 1; // The index of the newly created bin

                    finalPlacedItems[item.id] = item; // Store by original ID
                } else {
                    // This should ideally not happen if bin dimensions are larger than item
                    // For robustness, handle error or skip item
                    std::cerr << "Error: Could not place item " << item.id << " even in a new bin." << std::endl;
                }
            }
        }

        // Output results
        std::cout << bins.size() << std::endl;
        for (const auto& placedItem : finalPlacedItems) {
            std::cout << placedItem.binIndex << " " << placedItem.x << " " << placedItem.y << " " << placedItem.rotated << std::endl;
        }
    }

private:
    int boardWidth_;
    int boardHeight_;
};

int main() {
    std::ios_base::sync_with_stdio(false);
    std::cin.tie(NULL);

    int numRectangles, boardWidth, boardHeight;
    std::cin >> numRectangles >> boardWidth >> boardHeight;

    std::vector<Item> items;
    items.reserve(numRectangles);
    for (int i = 0; i < numRectangles; ++i) {
        int w, h;
        std::cin >> w >> h;
        items.emplace_back(i, w, h);
    }

    Solver solver(boardWidth, boardHeight);
    solver.solve(items);

    return 0;
}

Test 11: 2D Polygon Cutting Stock (Rust) Score: 0.00 / 11
Typical Prompt:
You are solving a 2D Polygon Cutting Stock Problem in Rust.

**The Challenge:**
Your program will be tested with problems ranging from 2 simple pieces to 5000+ complex polygons.
The same program must work efficiently across ALL scales.

**Input format (stdin):**
Line 1: SV NP (number of stock polygon vertices, number of pieces)
Next SV lines: x y (stock polygon vertices)
For each piece:
Line: PV (number of vertices in this piece)
Next PV lines: x y (piece polygon vertices)

**Output format (stdout):**
Line 1: num_stocks
Next NP lines: stock_index x y rotation (one per piece)
stock_index: 0-indexed stock polygon
x y: translation to apply
rotation: angle in radians

**Critical Requirements:**
1. **Scalability**: Your algorithm must adapt based on number and complexity of pieces
2. **Performance**: Must complete within 5 minutes even for 5000+ complex pieces
3. **Quality**: Minimize stock usage while ensuring valid placements

**Constraints:**
- Each placed piece must fit entirely within the stock polygon
- Pieces on same stock must not overlap
- Pieces may be rotated to any angle

**Environment:**
Platform: Windows 11 (build 10.0.26200)
Architecture: AMD64
CPU: AMD64 Family 23 Model 8 Stepping 2, AuthenticAMD
Core count: 64
Memory: 127.87 GB
SIMD: AVX, AVX2, SSE, SSE2, SSE3, SSE4.1, SSE4.2, SSSE3
SIMD detail: AVX=yes, AVX-512F=no, AVX2=yes, SSE=yes, SSE2=yes, SSE3=yes, SSE4.1=yes, SSE4.2=yes, SSSE3=yes

**Rust Compiler:**
rustc 1.92.0 (ded5c06cf 2025-12-08) | edition=2024 | host: x86_64-pc-windows-msvc

Write complete, compilable Rust code with a main() function.
 Prompt Changes:
Prompt parameters change between subpasses (increasing difficulty)
Subpass 0
Parameters: (see typical prompt)View exact prompt
View raw AI output
View chain of thought

Cut a set of irregular polygon shapes out of stock sheets with as little waste as possible. Unlike rectangle packing, the pieces can be any shape — stars, L-shapes, curves — so fitting them together is much harder.

The AI must handle polygon geometry (containment, intersection, rotation) and plan efficient layouts. Subpasses increase the number and complexity of the pieces. The baseline uses a simple greedy placement strategy.
