Polygon Cutting - 4 rectangles from 100x100 square

View Code (11129 chars)
use geo_types::{polygon, Point, Polygon}; // Point and Polygon are needed for transformations
use log::{info, LevelFilter};
use env_logger::Builder;
use u_nesting_d2::{Boundary2D, Config, Geometry2D, Nester2D, Strategy, Solver};
use svg::{Document, node::element::Path}; // Removed SvgPolygon
use svg::node::element::path::Data;
use std::collections::HashMap; // Import HashMap
use std::fs;

// Helper function to convert geo_types::Polygon to svg::node::element::Path
fn geo_polygon_to_svg_path(poly: &Polygon, fill_color: &str, stroke_color: &str, stroke_width: f64) -> Path {
    let mut data = Data::new();

    // Exterior ring
    let mut exterior_coords = poly.exterior().coords();
    if let Some(first_coord) = exterior_coords.next() {
        data = data.move_to((first_coord.x, first_coord.y));
        for coord in exterior_coords {
            data = data.line_to((coord.x, coord.y));
        }
        data = data.close();
    }

    // Interior rings (holes)
    for interior_ring in poly.interiors() {
        let mut interior_coords = interior_ring.coords();
        if let Some(first_coord) = interior_coords.next() {
            data = data.move_to((first_coord.x, first_coord.y));
            for coord in interior_coords {
                data = data.line_to((coord.x, coord.y));
            }
            data = data.close();
        }
    }

    Path::new()
        .set("fill", fill_color)
        .set("stroke", stroke_color)
        .set("stroke-width", stroke_width)
        .set("d", data)
}

// Function to apply translation and rotation to a geo_types::Polygon
fn transform_geo_polygon(poly: &Polygon, translate_x: f64, translate_y: f64, rotate_deg: f64) -> Polygon {
    let mut transformed_exterior = poly.exterior().clone();
    let mut transformed_interiors = poly.interiors().to_vec();

    // Apply rotation around the polygon's centroid or a chosen origin (e.g., 0,0)
    // For simplicity, let's rotate around (0,0) first, then translate.
    // This is not strictly correct for polygon's own center, but sufficient for visual.
    let rotate_rad = rotate_deg.to_radians();
    let cos_theta = rotate_rad.cos();
    let sin_theta = rotate_rad.sin();

    let transform_point = |p: Point| -> Point {
        let rotated_x = p.x() * cos_theta - p.y() * sin_theta;
        let rotated_y = p.x() * sin_theta + p.y() * cos_theta;
        Point::new(rotated_x + translate_x, rotated_y + translate_y)
    };

    transformed_exterior = transformed_exterior.points().map(transform_point).collect();
    transformed_interiors = transformed_interiors.into_iter().map(|ring| {
        ring.points().map(transform_point).collect()
    }).collect();

    Polygon::new(transformed_exterior, transformed_interiors)
}


fn main() {
    // Initialize logging
    Builder::new()
        .filter_level(LevelFilter::Info)
        .init();

    info!("Starting polygon nesting application...");

    // Define the stock polygon (a simple rectangle)
    let stock_polygon_geo = polygon![
        (x: 0.0, y: 0.0),
        (x: 100.0, y: 0.0),
        (x: 100.0, y: 100.0),
        (x: 0.0, y: 100.0),
        (x: 0.0, y: 0.0),
    ];
    info!("Stock polygon defined (geo_types): {:?}", stock_polygon_geo);

    // Define an L-shaped item polygon
    let item_polygon_geo_1 = polygon![
        (x: 0.0, y: 0.0),
        (x: 30.0, y: 0.0),
        (x: 30.0, y: 10.0),
        (x: 10.0, y: 10.0),
        (x: 10.0, y: 30.0),
        (x: 0.0, y: 30.0),
        (x: 0.0, y: 0.0),
    ];
    info!("Item polygon 1 defined (geo_types): {:?}", item_polygon_geo_1);

    // Define another L-shaped item polygon (can be a copy for now)
    let item_polygon_geo_2 = polygon![
        (x: 0.0, y: 0.0),
        (x: 30.0, y: 0.0),
        (x: 30.0, y: 10.0),
        (x: 10.0, y: 10.0),
        (x: 10.0, y: 30.0),
        (x: 0.0, y: 30.0),
        (x: 0.0, y: 0.0),
    ];
    info!("Item polygon 2 defined (geo_types): {:?}", item_polygon_geo_2);

    let item_polygons_geo = vec![
        ("item_0".to_string(), item_polygon_geo_1.clone()),
        ("item_1".to_string(), item_polygon_geo_2.clone()),
    ];

    // Convert geo_types::Polygon to u_nesting_d2::Geometry2D and Boundary2D
    // For stock, use the bounding box or convert its exterior to Boundary2D
    // u-nesting-d2's Boundary2D::rectangle is convenient.
    let boundary = Boundary2D::rectangle(100.0, 100.0); // Assuming 100x100 for our stock
    info!("Boundary defined for u-nesting-d2: {:?}", boundary);

    let mut items_nesting: Vec<Geometry2D> = Vec::new();
    for (id, poly_geo) in item_polygons_geo.iter() {
        let exterior_coords: Vec<(f64, f64)> = poly_geo.exterior().coords().map(|c| (c.x, c.y)).collect();
        // Assuming no interior rings for simplicity for now
        let item_nesting = Geometry2D::new(id)
            .with_polygon(exterior_coords)
            .with_rotations_deg(vec![0.0, 90.0, 180.0, 270.0]); // Allow 90-degree rotations
        items_nesting.push(item_nesting);
    }
    info!("Converted geo_types items to u-nesting-d2 Geometry2D items.");

    // Configure the Nester
    let config = Config::new()
        .with_strategy(Strategy::NfpGuided) // Revert to NfpGuided strategy
        .with_spacing(0.1) // Small gap between parts
        .with_margin(0.1) // Small margin from boundary edges
        .with_time_limit(60000); // Set a timeout of 60 seconds (60000 ms)

    let nester = Nester2D::new(config);
    info!("Nester2D configured with NfpGuided strategy and a time limit.");

    // Solve the nesting problem
    match nester.solve(&items_nesting, &boundary) {
        Ok(result) => {
            info!("Nesting Complete!");
            println!("Nesting Complete!");
            println!("Items Placed: {}", result.placements.len());
            println!("Material Utilization: {:.2}%", result.utilization * 100.0);

            let mut document = Document::new().set("viewBox", (0, 0, 100, 100)); // Set viewBox to match stock size

            // Add stock polygon to SVG
            let stock_svg = geo_polygon_to_svg_path(&stock_polygon_geo, "lightgray", "black", 0.5);
            document = document.add(stock_svg);

            // Create a hashmap for easy lookup of original geo_types polygons by their ID
            let original_item_polygons: HashMap<String, Polygon> = item_polygons_geo.into_iter().collect();

            // Add placed items to SVG
            for placement in result.placements {
                println!(
                    "Item: {} | Position: ({:.1}, {:.1}) | Rotation: {:?}°",
                    placement.geometry_id,
                    placement.position[0],
                    placement.position[1],
                    placement.rotation
                );

                if let Some(original_poly) = original_item_polygons.get(&placement.geometry_id) {
                    // u-nesting-d2's `placement.rotation` is a Vec<f64>.
                    // Assuming the first element is the rotation in degrees for now based on the previous output.
                    // This is a simplification and might need adjustment if rotation is more complex.
                    let rotation_deg = placement.rotation.first().copied().unwrap_or(0.0);

                    let transformed_poly = transform_geo_polygon(
                        original_poly,
                        placement.position[0],
                        placement.position[1],
                        rotation_deg,
                    );
                    let item_svg = geo_polygon_to_svg_path(&transformed_poly, "skyblue", "blue", 0.2);
                    document = document.add(item_svg);
                } else {
                    log::warn!("Original polygon not found for geometry_id: {}", placement.geometry_id);
                }
            }

            // Save SVG to file
            match fs::write("output.svg", document.to_string()) {
                Ok(_) => info!("SVG output saved to output.svg"),
                Err(e) => log::error!("Failed to save SVG: {:?}", e),
            }
        }
        Err(e) => {
            eprintln!("Nesting failed: {:?}", e);
            log::error!("Nesting failed: {:?}", e);
        },
    }

    println!("Hello, world! Polygons defined and nesting attempted.");
}

#[cfg(test)]
mod tests {
    use super::*;
    use geo_types::{polygon, Point};

    // Helper for floating point comparisons
    const EPSILON: f64 = 1e-9;

    #[test]
    fn test_transform_geo_polygon_translation() {
        let original_square = polygon![
            (x: 0.0, y: 0.0),
            (x: 10.0, y: 0.0),
            (x: 10.0, y: 10.0),
            (x: 0.0, y: 10.0),
            (x: 0.0, y: 0.0),
        ];

        let translated_square = transform_geo_polygon(&original_square, 5.0, 5.0, 0.0);

        // Check a single point after translation
        let first_point = translated_square.exterior().points().next().unwrap();
        assert!((first_point.x() - 5.0).abs() < EPSILON);
        assert!((first_point.y() - 5.0).abs() < EPSILON);

        // Check another point
        let second_point = translated_square.exterior().points().nth(1).unwrap();
        assert!((second_point.x() - 15.0).abs() < EPSILON);
        assert!((second_point.y() - 5.0).abs() < EPSILON);
    }

    #[test]
    fn test_transform_geo_polygon_rotation_90_deg() {
        let original_point = Point::new(1.0, 0.0);
        // Rotate (1,0) by 90 degrees around (0,0) -> (0,1)

        let square_for_rotation = polygon![
            (x: 0.0, y: 0.0),
            (x: 1.0, y: 0.0),
            (x: 1.0, y: 1.0),
            (x: 0.0, y: 1.0),
            (x: 0.0, y: 0.0),
        ];

        let rotated_square = transform_geo_polygon(&square_for_rotation, 0.0, 0.0, 90.0);

        let first_rotated_point = rotated_square.exterior().points().nth(1).unwrap(); // Original (1,0)
        assert!((first_rotated_point.x() - 0.0).abs() < EPSILON);
        assert!((first_rotated_point.y() - 1.0).abs() < EPSILON);

        let second_rotated_point = rotated_square.exterior().points().nth(2).unwrap(); // Original (1,1)
        assert!((second_rotated_point.x() - -1.0).abs() < EPSILON);
        assert!((second_rotated_point.y() - 1.0).abs() < EPSILON);
    }

    #[test]
    fn test_transform_geo_polygon_rotation_and_translation() {
        let original_square = polygon![
            (x: 0.0, y: 0.0),
            (x: 10.0, y: 0.0),
            (x: 10.0, y: 10.0),
            (x: 0.0, y: 10.0),
            (x: 0.0, y: 0.0),
        ];

        // Rotate by 90 degrees and then translate by (5,5)
        let transformed_square = transform_geo_polygon(&original_square, 5.0, 5.0, 90.0);

        // Original point (10,0) after 90 deg rotation around (0,0) is (0,10).
        // Then translate by (5,5) -> (5, 15)
        let expected_x_point_2 = 5.0;
        let expected_y_point_2 = 15.0;

        let transformed_point_2 = transformed_square.exterior().points().nth(1).unwrap();
        assert!((transformed_point_2.x() - expected_x_point_2).abs() < EPSILON);
        assert!((transformed_point_2.y() - expected_y_point_2).abs() < EPSILON);
    }
}

use geo_types::{polygon, Point, Polygon}; // Point and Polygon are needed for transformations
use log::{info, LevelFilter};
use env_logger::Builder;
use u_nesting_d2::{Boundary2D, Config, Geometry2D, Nester2D, Strategy, Solver};
use svg::{Document, node::element::Path}; // Removed SvgPolygon
use svg::node::element::path::Data;
use std::collections::HashMap; // Import HashMap
use std::fs;

// Helper function to convert geo_types::Polygon to svg::node::element::Path
fn geo_polygon_to_svg_path(poly: &Polygon, fill_color: &str, stroke_color: &str, stroke_width: f64) -> Path {
    let mut data = Data::new();

    // Exterior ring
    let mut exterior_coords = poly.exterior().coords();
    if let Some(first_coord) = exterior_coords.next() {
        data = data.move_to((first_coord.x, first_coord.y));
        for coord in exterior_coords {
            data = data.line_to((coord.x, coord.y));
        }
        data = data.close();
    }

    // Interior rings (holes)
    for interior_ring in poly.interiors() {
        let mut interior_coords = interior_ring.coords();
        if let Some(first_coord) = interior_coords.next() {
            data = data.move_to((first_coord.x, first_coord.y));
            for coord in interior_coords {
                data = data.line_to((coord.x, coord.y));
            }
            data = data.close();
        }
    }

    Path::new()
        .set("fill", fill_color)
        .set("stroke", stroke_color)
        .set("stroke-width", stroke_width)
        .set("d", data)
}

// Function to apply translation and rotation to a geo_types::Polygon
fn transform_geo_polygon(poly: &Polygon, translate_x: f64, translate_y: f64, rotate_deg: f64) -> Polygon {
    let mut transformed_exterior = poly.exterior().clone();
    let mut transformed_interiors = poly.interiors().to_vec();

    // Apply rotation around the polygon's centroid or a chosen origin (e.g., 0,0)
    // For simplicity, let's rotate around (0,0) first, then translate.
    // This is not strictly correct for polygon's own center, but sufficient for visual.
    let rotate_rad = rotate_deg.to_radians();
    let cos_theta = rotate_rad.cos();
    let sin_theta = rotate_rad.sin();

    let transform_point = |p: Point| -> Point {
        let rotated_x = p.x() * cos_theta - p.y() * sin_theta;
        let rotated_y = p.x() * sin_theta + p.y() * cos_theta;
        Point::new(rotated_x + translate_x, rotated_y + translate_y)
    };

    transformed_exterior = transformed_exterior.points().map(transform_point).collect();
    transformed_interiors = transformed_interiors.into_iter().map(|ring| {
        ring.points().map(transform_point).collect()
    }).collect();

    Polygon::new(transformed_exterior, transformed_interiors)
}


fn main() {
    // Initialize logging
    Builder::new()
        .filter_level(LevelFilter::Info)
        .init();

    info!("Starting polygon nesting application...");

    // Define the stock polygon (a simple rectangle)
    let stock_polygon_geo = polygon![
        (x: 0.0, y: 0.0),
        (x: 100.0, y: 0.0),
        (x: 100.0, y: 100.0),
        (x: 0.0, y: 100.0),
        (x: 0.0, y: 0.0),
    ];
    info!("Stock polygon defined (geo_types): {:?}", stock_polygon_geo);

    // Define an L-shaped item polygon
    let item_polygon_geo_1 = polygon![
        (x: 0.0, y: 0.0),
        (x: 30.0, y: 0.0),
        (x: 30.0, y: 10.0),
        (x: 10.0, y: 10.0),
        (x: 10.0, y: 30.0),
        (x: 0.0, y: 30.0),
        (x: 0.0, y: 0.0),
    ];
    info!("Item polygon 1 defined (geo_types): {:?}", item_polygon_geo_1);

    // Define another L-shaped item polygon (can be a copy for now)
    let item_polygon_geo_2 = polygon![
        (x: 0.0, y: 0.0),
        (x: 30.0, y: 0.0),
        (x: 30.0, y: 10.0),
        (x: 10.0, y: 10.0),
        (x: 10.0, y: 30.0),
        (x: 0.0, y: 30.0),
        (x: 0.0, y: 0.0),
    ];
    info!("Item polygon 2 defined (geo_types): {:?}", item_polygon_geo_2);

    let item_polygons_geo = vec![
        ("item_0".to_string(), item_polygon_geo_1.clone()),
        ("item_1".to_string(), item_polygon_geo_2.clone()),
    ];

    // Convert geo_types::Polygon to u_nesting_d2::Geometry2D and Boundary2D
    // For stock, use the bounding box or convert its exterior to Boundary2D
    // u-nesting-d2's Boundary2D::rectangle is convenient.
    let boundary = Boundary2D::rectangle(100.0, 100.0); // Assuming 100x100 for our stock
    info!("Boundary defined for u-nesting-d2: {:?}", boundary);

    let mut items_nesting: Vec<Geometry2D> = Vec::new();
    for (id, poly_geo) in item_polygons_geo.iter() {
        let exterior_coords: Vec<(f64, f64)> = poly_geo.exterior().coords().map(|c| (c.x, c.y)).collect();
        // Assuming no interior rings for simplicity for now
        let item_nesting = Geometry2D::new(id)
            .with_polygon(exterior_coords)
            .with_rotations_deg(vec![0.0, 90.0, 180.0, 270.0]); // Allow 90-degree rotations
        items_nesting.push(item_nesting);
    }
    info!("Converted geo_types items to u-nesting-d2 Geometry2D items.");

    // Configure the Nester
    let config = Config::new()
        .with_strategy(Strategy::NfpGuided) // Revert to NfpGuided strategy
        .with_spacing(0.1) // Small gap between parts
        .with_margin(0.1) // Small margin from boundary edges
        .with_time_limit(60000); // Set a timeout of 60 seconds (60000 ms)

    let nester = Nester2D::new(config);
    info!("Nester2D configured with NfpGuided strategy and a time limit.");

    // Solve the nesting problem
    match nester.solve(&items_nesting, &boundary) {
        Ok(result) => {
            info!("Nesting Complete!");
            println!("Nesting Complete!");
            println!("Items Placed: {}", result.placements.len());
            println!("Material Utilization: {:.2}%", result.utilization * 100.0);

            let mut document = Document::new().set("viewBox", (0, 0, 100, 100)); // Set viewBox to match stock size

            // Add stock polygon to SVG
            let stock_svg = geo_polygon_to_svg_path(&stock_polygon_geo, "lightgray", "black", 0.5);
            document = document.add(stock_svg);

            // Create a hashmap for easy lookup of original geo_types polygons by their ID
            let original_item_polygons: HashMap<String, Polygon> = item_polygons_geo.into_iter().collect();

            // Add placed items to SVG
            for placement in result.placements {
                println!(
                    "Item: {} | Position: ({:.1}, {:.1}) | Rotation: {:?}°",
                    placement.geometry_id,
                    placement.position[0],
                    placement.position[1],
                    placement.rotation
                );

                if let Some(original_poly) = original_item_polygons.get(&placement.geometry_id) {
                    // u-nesting-d2's `placement.rotation` is a Vec<f64>.
                    // Assuming the first element is the rotation in degrees for now based on the previous output.
                    // This is a simplification and might need adjustment if rotation is more complex.
                    let rotation_deg = placement.rotation.first().copied().unwrap_or(0.0);

                    let transformed_poly = transform_geo_polygon(
                        original_poly,
                        placement.position[0],
                        placement.position[1],
                        rotation_deg,
                    );
                    let item_svg = geo_polygon_to_svg_path(&transformed_poly, "skyblue", "blue", 0.2);
                    document = document.add(item_svg);
                } else {
                    log::warn!("Original polygon not found for geometry_id: {}", placement.geometry_id);
                }
            }

            // Save SVG to file
            match fs::write("output.svg", document.to_string()) {
                Ok(_) => info!("SVG output saved to output.svg"),
                Err(e) => log::error!("Failed to save SVG: {:?}", e),
            }
        }
        Err(e) => {
            eprintln!("Nesting failed: {:?}", e);
            log::error!("Nesting failed: {:?}", e);
        },
    }

    println!("Hello, world! Polygons defined and nesting attempted.");
}

#[cfg(test)]
mod tests {
    use super::*;
    use geo_types::{polygon, Point};

    // Helper for floating point comparisons
    const EPSILON: f64 = 1e-9;

    #[test]
    fn test_transform_geo_polygon_translation() {
        let original_square = polygon![
            (x: 0.0, y: 0.0),
            (x: 10.0, y: 0.0),
            (x: 10.0, y: 10.0),
            (x: 0.0, y: 10.0),
            (x: 0.0, y: 0.0),
        ];

        let translated_square = transform_geo_polygon(&original_square, 5.0, 5.0, 0.0);

        // Check a single point after translation
        let first_point = translated_square.exterior().points().next().unwrap();
        assert!((first_point.x() - 5.0).abs() < EPSILON);
        assert!((first_point.y() - 5.0).abs() < EPSILON);

        // Check another point
        let second_point = translated_square.exterior().points().nth(1).unwrap();
        assert!((second_point.x() - 15.0).abs() < EPSILON);
        assert!((second_point.y() - 5.0).abs() < EPSILON);
    }

    #[test]
    fn test_transform_geo_polygon_rotation_90_deg() {
        let original_point = Point::new(1.0, 0.0);
        // Rotate (1,0) by 90 degrees around (0,0) -> (0,1)

        let square_for_rotation = polygon![
            (x: 0.0, y: 0.0),
            (x: 1.0, y: 0.0),
            (x: 1.0, y: 1.0),
            (x: 0.0, y: 1.0),
            (x: 0.0, y: 0.0),
        ];

        let rotated_square = transform_geo_polygon(&square_for_rotation, 0.0, 0.0, 90.0);

        let first_rotated_point = rotated_square.exterior().points().nth(1).unwrap(); // Original (1,0)
        assert!((first_rotated_point.x() - 0.0).abs() < EPSILON);
        assert!((first_rotated_point.y() - 1.0).abs() < EPSILON);

        let second_rotated_point = rotated_square.exterior().points().nth(2).unwrap(); // Original (1,1)
        assert!((second_rotated_point.x() - -1.0).abs() < EPSILON);
        assert!((second_rotated_point.y() - 1.0).abs() < EPSILON);
    }

    #[test]
    fn test_transform_geo_polygon_rotation_and_translation() {
        let original_square = polygon![
            (x: 0.0, y: 0.0),
            (x: 10.0, y: 0.0),
            (x: 10.0, y: 10.0),
            (x: 0.0, y: 10.0),
            (x: 0.0, y: 0.0),
        ];

        // Rotate by 90 degrees and then translate by (5,5)
        let transformed_square = transform_geo_polygon(&original_square, 5.0, 5.0, 90.0);

        // Original point (10,0) after 90 deg rotation around (0,0) is (0,10).
        // Then translate by (5,5) -> (5, 15)
        let expected_x_point_2 = 5.0;
        let expected_y_point_2 = 15.0;

        let transformed_point_2 = transformed_square.exterior().points().nth(1).unwrap();
        assert!((transformed_point_2.x() - expected_x_point_2).abs() < EPSILON);
        assert!((transformed_point_2.y() - expected_y_point_2).abs() < EPSILON);
    }
}

Test 12: 3D Bin Packing - Polyhedra in Box (C#) Score: 0.00 / 11
Typical Prompt:
You are solving a 3D Bin Packing problem in C#.

You must write a C# solver that can handle ANY problem size from trivial to ludicrous scale:
- **Trivial**: Small containers (5x5x5), simple polyhedra
- **Medium**: Medium containers (10x10x10-20x20x20)
- **Large**: Large containers (50x50x50-100x100x100)
- **Extreme**: Massive containers (200x200x200-500x500x500)

**Input format (stdin):**
Line 1: V F (vertex/face count of the polyhedron)
Next V lines: x y z (polyhedron vertices)
Next F lines: a b c (polyhedron face indices)
Last line: W H D (container width, height, depth)
Container origin at (0,0,0), extends to (W,H,D).

**Output format (stdout):**
Line 1: count (number of polyhedra packed)
Next count lines: tx ty tz qw qx qy qz
tx,ty,tz: translation
qw,qx,qy,qz: rotation quaternion (w is scalar part)

**Quaternion Rotation Reference:**
- Identity (no rotation): 1 0 0 0
- 90° around Z: 0.707 0 0 0.707
- To rotate a point: p' = q * p * q^(-1)

**Critical Requirements:**
1. **Scalability**: Your algorithm must adapt based on container size and polyhedron complexity
2. **Performance**: Must complete within 5 minutes even for massive containers
3. **Quality**: Maximize packing count while ensuring valid placements

**Environment:**
Platform: Windows 11 (build 10.0.26200)
Architecture: AMD64
CPU: AMD64 Family 23 Model 8 Stepping 2, AuthenticAMD
Core count: 64
Memory: 127.87 GB
SIMD: AVX, AVX2, SSE, SSE2, SSE3, SSE4.1, SSE4.2, SSSE3
SIMD detail: AVX=yes, AVX-512F=no, AVX2=yes, SSE=yes, SSE2=yes, SSE3=yes, SSE4.1=yes, SSE4.2=yes, SSSE3=yes

**C# Compiler:**

The AI must handle 3D collision detection and spatial reasoning. Subpasses use increasingly awkward shapes that resist neat stacking. The baseline uses a simple grid placement without rotation optimisation.

Test 13: Longest Common Substring (C++) Score: 0.00 / 12
Typical Prompt:
You are solving the Longest Common Substring problem in C++.

You must write a C++ solver that can handle ANY problem size from trivial to ludicrous scale:
- **Trivial**: 2-3 short strings (10-50 chars each)
- **Medium**: 3-5 medium strings (100-500 chars each)
- **Large**: 5-10 long strings (1000-5000 chars each)
- **Extreme**: 100 very long strings (10MB+ each), massive search space

**Input format (stdin):**
Line 1: N (number of strings)
Next N lines: one string per line

**Output format (stdout):**
One line: the longest common substring
If no common substring exists, output an empty line.

**Example:**
Input:
3
abcdef
xbcdey
zbcdew

Output:
bcde

**Critical Requirements:**
1. **Scalability**: Your algorithm must adapt based on number and length of strings
2. **Performance**: Must complete within 300 seconds even for very long strings
3. **Correctness**: Must find the actual longest common substring

**Environment:**
Platform: Windows 11 (build 10.0.26200)
Architecture: AMD64
CPU: AMD64 Family 23 Model 8 Stepping 2, AuthenticAMD
Core count: 64
Memory: 127.87 GB
SIMD: AVX, AVX2, SSE, SSE2, SSE3, SSE4.1, SSE4.2, SSSE3
SIMD detail: AVX=yes, AVX-512F=no, AVX2=yes, SSE=yes, SSE2=yes, SSE3=yes, SSE4.1=yes, SSE4.2=yes, SSSE3=yes

**C++ Compiler:**
MSVC cl 19.50.35730 | std=c++20 (/std:c++20)

Be sure that any deviation from the C++ standard library is supported by the given compiler,
as referencing the wrong intrinsics or non-standard header like 'bits/stdc++.h' could fail your submission.

Write complete, compilable C++ code with a main() function.
Include adaptive logic that chooses different strategies based on problem scale.
 Prompt Changes:
Prompt parameters change between subpasses (increasing difficulty)
Subpass 0
Parameters: (see typical prompt)View exact prompt
View raw AI output
View chain of thought

Given several strings, find the longest chunk of text that appears identically in every one of them. For example, if the strings are “abcdef” and “xbcdey”, the answer is “bcde”.

Naïve approaches check every possible substring and become far too slow on large inputs. Subpasses increase the string lengths into the millions, so the AI must use efficient data structures or hashing. The baseline uses a simple library call that times out on the bigger cases.

 0.0000
[3 short strings (7 chars)] Unable to open question.txt

Longest Common Substring - 3 short strings (7 chars)

View Code (1480 chars)
#include <iostream>
#include <string>
#include <vector>
#include <algorithm>
#include <fstream>

std::string longestCommonSubstring(const std::string& s1, const std::string& s2) {
    int m = s1.length();
    int n = s2.length();
    
    std::vector<std::vector<int>> dp(m + 1, std::vector<int>(n + 1, 0));
    int maxLength = 0;
    int endIndex = 0;

    for (int i = 1; i <= m; ++i) {
        for (int j = 1; j <= n; ++j) {
            if (s1[i - 1] == s2[j - 1]) {
                dp[i][j] = dp[i - 1][j - 1] + 1;
                if (dp[i][j] > maxLength) {
                    maxLength = dp[i][j];
                    endIndex = i - 1;
                }
            } else {
                dp[i][j] = 0;
            }
        }
    }
    
    if (maxLength == 0) {
        return "";
    }
    return s1.substr(endIndex - maxLength + 1, maxLength);
}

int main() {
    std::ifstream inputFile("question.txt");
    std::string s1, s2;

    if (inputFile.is_open()) {
        std::getline(inputFile, s1);
        std::getline(inputFile, s2);
        inputFile.close();
    } else {
        std::cerr << "Unable to open question.txt" << std::endl;
        return 1;
    }

    std::string lcs = longestCommonSubstring(s1, s2);

    std::ofstream outputFile("answer.txt");
    if (outputFile.is_open()) {
        outputFile << lcs;
        outputFile.close();
    } else {
        std::cerr << "Unable to open answer.txt" << std::endl;
        return 1;
    }

    return 0;
}

Test 14: Minesweeper Solver (C#) Score: 0.00 / 10
Typical Prompt:
You are writing a Minesweeper solver in C# that must handle ANY board complexity from trivial to ludicrous scale.

- **Trivial**: Small boards (5x5-8x8), simple patterns, few mines
- **Extreme**: Massive boards (10000x10000), very complex constraint satisfaction problems

**Input format (stdin):**
Line 1: H W (height and width)
Next H lines: the board row strings (each W characters)

**Cell meanings:**
- ' ' (space): Unknown/unrevealed cell - could be mine or safe
- '0'-'8': Revealed safe cell showing count of adjacent mines (8 neighbors)
- '*': Flagged mine (known mine location)

**Output format (stdout):**
Line 1: N (number of safe cells found)
Next N lines: x y (column row, 0-indexed)
Only output cells that are currently unknown (' ') and provably safe.
Do NOT guess - only return cells you can prove are safe.

**Example:**
For a board where cell (0,0) shows '1' and has one adjacent mine at (1,0):
Neighbors (0,1) and (0,2) are guaranteed safe.

**Constraints:**
- Return as many provably safe cells as possible
- Never return a cell that could be a mine
- Must handle varying board sizes and mine densities efficiently

**Environment:**
Platform: Windows 11 (build 10.0.26200)
Architecture: AMD64
CPU: AMD64 Family 23 Model 8 Stepping 2, AuthenticAMD
Core count: 64
Memory: 127.87 GB
SIMD: AVX, AVX2, SSE, SSE2, SSE3, SSE4.1, SSE4.2, SSSE3
SIMD detail: AVX=yes, AVX-512F=no, AVX2=yes, SSE=yes, SSE2=yes, SSE3=yes, SSE4.1=yes, SSE4.2=yes, SSSE3=yes

**C# Compiler:**

Include adaptive logic that chooses different strategies based on board complexity.

Given a partially-revealed Minesweeper board, deduce which hidden cells are safe to click and which contain mines. Each visible number tells you exactly how many of its neighbours are mined — the AI must combine these clues to make guaranteed-safe moves without guessing.

Larger boards and denser mine layouts make the logic harder. The problem is NP-complete in general, but clever constraint reasoning can solve most practical boards. The baseline uses only the simplest single-cell deduction rules.

Test 15: Tetrahedron Shadow Covering (Rust) Score: 0.00 / 11
Typical Prompt:
You are solving a Shadow Covering problem with tetrahedrons in Rust.

**The Challenge:**
Your program will be tested with polygons ranging from simple shapes to very complex geometries.
The same program must work efficiently across ALL scales.

**Input format (stdin):**
Line 1: NV (number of polygon vertices)
Next NV lines: x y (polygon vertices on z=0 plane)
Last line: sx sy sz (sun direction vector, points from sun toward ground)

**Output format (stdout):**
Line 1: count (number of tetrahedrons)
Next count lines: px py pz qw qx qy qz scale
px,py,pz: center position
qw,qx,qy,qz: rotation quaternion (w is scalar)
scale: uniform scale factor

**Standard Tetrahedron Reference:**
Vertices (before transform):
[1.0, 0.0, -0.707]
[-1.0, 0.0, -0.707]
[0.0, 1.0, 0.707]
[0.0, -1.0, 0.707]

**Constraints:**
- Combined shadows must completely cover target polygon
- Tetrahedrons must not intersect each other in 3D
- Maximum scale factor per tetrahedron: 10.0
- Minimize number of tetrahedrons

**Environment:**
Platform: Windows 11 (build 10.0.26200)
Architecture: AMD64
CPU: AMD64 Family 23 Model 8 Stepping 2, AuthenticAMD
Core count: 64
Memory: 127.87 GB
SIMD: AVX, AVX2, SSE, SSE2, SSE3, SSE4.1, SSE4.2, SSSE3
SIMD detail: AVX=yes, AVX-512F=no, AVX2=yes, SSE=yes, SSE2=yes, SSE3=yes, SSE4.1=yes, SSE4.2=yes, SSSE3=yes

**Rust Compiler:**
rustc 1.92.0 (ded5c06cf 2025-12-08) | edition=2024 | host: x86_64-pc-windows-msvc

Be aware that default warnings are enabled and will cause a compilation failure,
so ensure that you write warning-free code.

Include adaptive logic that chooses different strategies based on problem scale.

Place 3D tetrahedra (triangular pyramids) above a flat surface so that their shadows, cast by a directional light, completely cover a given 2D target shape. Use as few tetrahedra as possible while covering every part of the target.

The AI must reason about 3D-to-2D projection, rotation, and coverage geometry. Subpasses use more complex target shapes and trickier light angles. The baseline uses a simple grid of tetrahedra above the target.

Test 16: Job-Shop Scheduling (C++) Score: 0.00 / 14
Typical Prompt:
You are solving a Job-Shop Scheduling problem in C++.

You must write a C++ solver that can handle ANY problem size from trivial to extremely large scale:
- **Trivial**: 2-3 jobs, 2-3 machines, 5-10 tasks total
- **Extreme**: 1000+ jobs, 50+ machines, 50k+ tasks total

**Input format (stdin):**
Line 1: J M (number of jobs, number of machines)
For each job:
Line: T (number of tasks in this job)
Next T lines: machine duration

**Output format (stdout):**
For each job (J lines total):
T pairs per line: start_time machine start_time machine ... (all tasks for that job)

**Example:**
Input:
2 2
2
0 3
1 2
2
1 2
0 4

Output:
0 0 3 1
0 1 5 0

(Job 0: task 0 starts at t=0 on M0, task 1 at t=3 on M1)
(Job 1: task 0 starts at t=0 on M1, task 1 at t=5 on M0)

**Key Constraints:**
1. Each task in a job must run on its specified machine
2. Tasks within a job must execute in order (task i before task i+1)
3. A machine can only run one task at a time
4. Tasks cannot be interrupted once started

**Environment:**
Platform: Windows 11 (build 10.0.26200)
Architecture: AMD64
CPU: AMD64 Family 23 Model 8 Stepping 2, AuthenticAMD
Core count: 64
Memory: 127.87 GB
SIMD: AVX, AVX2, SSE, SSE2, SSE3, SSE4.1, SSE4.2, SSSE3
SIMD detail: AVX=yes, AVX-512F=no, AVX2=yes, SSE=yes, SSE2=yes, SSE3=yes, SSE4.1=yes, SSE4.2=yes, SSSE3=yes

**C++ Compiler:**
MSVC cl 19.50.35730 | std=c++20 (/std:c++20)

Write complete, compilable C++ code with a main() function.
Include adaptive logic that chooses different strategies based on problem scale.
 Prompt Changes:
Prompt parameters change between subpasses (increasing difficulty)
Subpass 0
Parameters: (see typical prompt)View exact prompt
View raw AI output
View chain of thought

Schedule a set of jobs across several machines to finish everything as quickly as possible. Each job is a sequence of tasks that must run in order, and each task needs a specific machine — but a machine can only do one thing at a time.

This classic factory-scheduling problem is NP-hard. Subpasses increase the number of jobs and machines. The baseline greedily starts whichever task can begin earliest.

 0.0000
[3 jobs, 2 machines] Compilation failed:
tmpgex7dn19.cpp
C:\Users\Ash\AppData\Local\Temp\tmpgex7dn19.cpp(106): error C2001: newline in constant
C:\Users\Ash\AppData\Local\Temp\tmpgex7dn19.cpp(106): error C2105: '--' needs

Job-Shop Scheduling - 3 jobs, 2 machines

View Code (6007 chars)
#include <iostream>
#include <vector>
#include <algorithm>
#include <queue>
#include <map>

// Represents an individual operation
struct Operation {
    int machine_id;
    int duration;
    int job_id;
    int operation_idx; // Index of this operation within its job's sequence
    int start_time = -1;
    int end_time = -1;
};

// Represents a job, which is a sequence of operations
struct Job {
    int id;
    std::vector<Operation> operations;
    int next_op_idx = 0; // Pointer to the next operation to be scheduled for this job
};

// Function to solve JSSP using a greedy SPT heuristic
void solveJSSP(int num_machines, std::vector<Job>& jobs) {
    int num_jobs = jobs.size();
    
    // machine_free_time[m] stores the time when machine 'm' becomes free
    std::vector<int> machine_free_time(num_machines, 0);
    // job_next_free_time[j] stores the time when job 'j' can start its next operation
    std::vector<int> job_next_free_time(num_jobs, 0);

    int completed_ops_count = 0;
    int total_ops = 0;
    for (const auto& job : jobs) {
        total_ops += job.operations.size();
    }

    std::cout << "Starting JSSP scheduling with " << num_jobs << " jobs and " << num_machines << " machines." << std::endl;

    // Loop until all operations are scheduled
    while (completed_ops_count < total_ops) {
        int best_job_to_schedule = -1;
        int earliest_possible_start_time = -1;
        int shortest_duration = -1;

        // Iterate through all jobs to find the next operation to schedule
        for (int i = 0; i < num_jobs; ++i) {
            // Check if job 'i' has unscheduled operations
            if (jobs[i].next_op_idx < jobs[i].operations.size()) {
                Operation& current_op = jobs[i].operations[jobs[i].next_op_idx];
                
                // Calculate the earliest time this operation can start
                // It must wait for its machine to be free AND for its job's previous operation to finish
                int current_op_start_time = std::max(machine_free_time[current_op.machine_id], job_next_free_time[i]);

                // Heuristic: Shortest Processing Time (SPT)
                // Select the operation that can start earliest, and among those, the one with the shortest duration.
                if (best_job_to_schedule == -1 || 
                    current_op_start_time < earliest_possible_start_time ||
                    (current_op_start_time == earliest_possible_start_time && current_op.duration < shortest_duration)) 
                {
                    earliest_possible_start_time = current_op_start_time;
                    shortest_duration = current_op.duration;
                    best_job_to_schedule = i;
                }
            }
        }

        // If an operation was selected, schedule it
        if (best_job_to_schedule != -1) {
            Job& job_to_update = jobs[best_job_to_schedule];
            Operation& op_to_schedule = job_to_update.operations[job_to_update.next_op_idx];

            op_to_schedule.start_time = earliest_possible_start_time;
            op_to_schedule.end_time = op_to_schedule.start_time + op_to_schedule.duration;

            // Update machine and job free times
            machine_free_time[op_to_schedule.machine_id] = op_to_schedule.end_time;
            job_next_free_time[best_job_to_schedule] = op_to_schedule.end_time;
            
            // Move to the next operation for this job
            job_to_update.next_op_idx++;
            completed_ops_count++;
            
            std::cout << "Scheduled Job " << op_to_schedule.job_id 
                      << " Op " << op_to_schedule.operation_idx 
                      << " on Machine " << op_to_schedule.machine_id 
                      << " from " << op_to_schedule.start_time 
                      << " to " << op_to_schedule.end_time 
                      << " (Duration: " << op_to_schedule.duration << ")" << std::endl;
        } else {
            // This case should ideally not happen if total_ops is correctly calculated and there are unscheduled ops
            std::cerr << "Error: No operation could be scheduled. This indicates a problem in the logic or input." << std::endl;
            break;
        }
    }

    // Calculate Makespan
    int makespan = 0;
    for (int time : machine_free_time) {
        if (time > makespan) {
            makespan = time;
        }
    }
    std::cout << "
--- Scheduling Complete ---" << std::endl;
    std::cout << "Final Makespan: " << makespan << std::endl;

    // Optional: Print detailed schedule
    std::cout << "
Detailed Schedule:" << std::endl;
    for (const auto& job : jobs) {
        std::cout << "Job " << job.id << ":" << std::endl;
        for (const auto& op : job.operations) {
            std::cout << "  Operation " << op.operation_idx 
                      << " (Machine " << op.machine_id 
                      << "): Start=" << op.start_time 
                      << ", End=" << op.end_time 
                      << ", Duration=" << op.duration << std::endl;
        }
    }
}

int main() {
    // Example JSSP Instance: (taken from the search result and slightly modified for clarity)
    // 2 Jobs, 2 Machines
    // Job 0: Operation 0 (Machine 0, Duration 3), Operation 1 (Machine 1, Duration 2)
    // Job 1: Operation 0 (Machine 1, Duration 2), Operation 1 (Machine 0, Duration 4)

    // Define operations for Job 0
    std::vector<Operation> job0_ops;
    job0_ops.push_back({0, 3, 0, 0}); // machine_id, duration, job_id, operation_idx
    job0_ops.push_back({1, 2, 0, 1});

    // Define operations for Job 1
    std::vector<Operation> job1_ops;
    job1_ops.push_back({1, 2, 1, 0});
    job1_ops.push_back({0, 4, 1, 1});

    // Create Job objects
    std::vector<Job> jobs;
    jobs.push_back({0, job0_ops});
    jobs.push_back({1, job1_ops});

    int num_machines = 2; // Total number of machines available

    // Solve the JSSP
    solveJSSP(num_machines, jobs);

    return 0;
}

Test 17: 3D AABB Bin Packing (C#) Score: 0.00 / 8
Typical Prompt:
You are solving a 3D Bin Packing problem with axis-aligned boxes in C#.

Your program must work on all scales, from 10,000 boxes in 1,000,000 cubic units
down to 4 boxes in a 10x10x10 container.

**Input format (stdin):**
Line 1: N W H D (number of boxes, container width, height, depth)
Next N lines: w h d (box dimensions)

**Output format (stdout):**
Line 1: packed_count
Next packed_count lines: box_index x y z (0-indexed box index and corner position)

**Critical Requirements:**
1. **Scalability**: Your algorithm must adapt based on container size and number of boxes
2. **Performance**: Must complete within 30 seconds even for very large containers
3. **Quality**: Maximize number of boxes packed while ensuring valid placements

**Constraints:**
- Boxes must be entirely within container bounds
- No two boxes may overlap
- Boxes are axis-aligned (no arbitrary rotation, but you may reorder w/h/d)
- Maximize number of boxes packed

**Environment:**
Platform: Windows 11 (build 10.0.26200)
Architecture: AMD64
CPU: AMD64 Family 23 Model 8 Stepping 2, AuthenticAMD
Core count: 64
Memory: 127.87 GB
SIMD: AVX, AVX2, SSE, SSE2, SSE3, SSE4.1, SSE4.2, SSSE3
SIMD detail: AVX=yes, AVX-512F=no, AVX2=yes, SSE=yes, SSE2=yes, SSE3=yes, SSE4.1=yes, SSE4.2=yes, SSSE3=yes

**C# Compiler:**
C# (csc.exe 5.5.0-2.26208.13 (3dc781932f83b0792b0d1717a68db3f9604a2cb1)) | max LangVersion 14

Write complete, compilable C# code with a static void Main method.
 Prompt Changes:
Prompt parameters change between subpasses (increasing difficulty)
Subpass 0
Parameters: (see typical prompt)View exact prompt
View raw AI output
View chain of thought

Pack a collection of rectangular boxes into a shipping container, fitting as much total volume in as possible. Boxes may be rotated to any axis-aligned orientation, but cannot overlap or stick out of the container.

This 3D packing problem is NP-hard. Subpasses increase the number and variety of box sizes. The baseline uses a greedy first-fit approach.

[4 boxes in 10x10x10] Compilation failed:
C:\Users\Ash\AppData\Local\Temp\tmp6y6g5iqb.cs(1,7): error CS0246: The type or namespace name 'NUnit' could not be found (are you missing a using directive or an assembly reference

3D AABB Packing - 4 boxes in 10x10x10

View Code (8073 chars)
using NUnit.Framework;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Linq;
using System;

[TestFixture]
public class BinPackerTests
{
    private BinPacker _packer;

    [SetUp]
    public void Setup()
    {
        _packer = new BinPacker();
    }

    [Test]
    public void Pack_SingleBoxPerfectFit_ReturnsAllPacked()
    {
        var bin = new Bin(10, 10, 10);
        var box = new Box("Box1", 10, 10, 10);
        var boxesToPack = new List<Box> { box };

        var result = _packer.Pack(bin, boxesToPack);

        Assert.That(result.AllBoxesPacked, Is.True);
        Assert.That(result.PackedBoxes.Count, Is.EqualTo(1));
        Assert.That(result.PackedBoxes[0].Id, Is.EqualTo("Box1"));
        Assert.That(result.PackedBoxes[0].X, Is.EqualTo(0));
        Assert.That(result.PackedBoxes[0].Y, Is.EqualTo(0));
        Assert.That(result.PackedBoxes[0].Z, Is.EqualTo(0));
        Assert.That(result.UnpackedBoxes, Is.Empty);
        Assert.That(result.UtilizedVolumePercentage, Is.EqualTo(100.0).Within(0.001));
    }

    [Test]
    public void Pack_MultipleBoxesPerfectFit_ReturnsAllPacked()
    {
        var bin = new Bin(20, 10, 10);
        var boxesToPack = new List<Box>
        {
            new Box("BoxA", 10, 10, 10),
            new Box("BoxB", 10, 10, 10)
        };

        var result = _packer.Pack(bin, boxesToPack);

        Assert.That(result.AllBoxesPacked, Is.True);
        Assert.That(result.PackedBoxes.Count, Is.EqualTo(2));
        Assert.That(result.UnpackedBoxes, Is.Empty);
        Assert.That(result.UtilizedVolumePercentage, Is.EqualTo(100.0).Within(0.001));

        // Verify positions (order might vary based on sorting and space splitting)
        var boxA = result.PackedBoxes.First(b => b.Id == "BoxA");
        var boxB = result.PackedBoxes.First(b => b.Id == "BoxB");

        // Assuming a simple packing along X-axis
        Assert.That(boxA.X == 0 && boxB.X == 10 || boxA.X == 10 && boxB.X == 0, "Boxes should be side-by-side along X");
        Assert.That(boxA.Y, Is.EqualTo(0));
        Assert.That(boxA.Z, Is.EqualTo(0));
        Assert.That(boxB.Y, Is.EqualTo(0));
        Assert.That(boxB.Z, Is.EqualTo(0));
    }

    [Test]
    public void Pack_BoxTooLargeForBin_ReturnsUnpackedBox()
    {
        var bin = new Bin(10, 10, 10);
        var box = new Box("LargeBox", 11, 10, 10); // Too long
        var boxesToPack = new List<Box> { box };

        var result = _packer.Pack(bin, boxesToPack);

        Assert.That(result.AllBoxesPacked, Is.False);
        Assert.That(result.PackedBoxes, Is.Empty);
        Assert.That(result.UnpackedBoxes.Count, Is.EqualTo(1));
        Assert.That(result.UnpackedBoxes[0].Id, Is.EqualTo("LargeBox"));
        Assert.That(result.UtilizedVolumePercentage, Is.EqualTo(0.0).Within(0.001));
    }

    [Test]
    public void Pack_SomeBoxesFitOthersDont_ReturnsPartialPacking()
    {
        var bin = new Bin(10, 10, 10);
        var boxesToPack = new List<Box>
        {
            new Box("SmallBox", 5, 5, 5),
            new Box("MediumBox", 6, 6, 6), // Should fit
            new Box("LargeBox", 11, 1, 1)  // Too large
        };

        var result = _packer.Pack(bin, boxesToPack);

        Assert.That(result.AllBoxesPacked, Is.False);
        Assert.That(result.PackedBoxes.Count, Is.EqualTo(2));
        Assert.That(result.UnpackedBoxes.Count, Is.EqualTo(1));
        Assert.That(result.UnpackedBoxes.Any(b => b.Id == "LargeBox"), Is.True);
        Assert.That(result.PackedBoxes.Any(b => b.Id == "SmallBox"), Is.True);
        Assert.That(result.PackedBoxes.Any(b => b.Id == "MediumBox"), Is.True);
    }
    
    // Test SplitSpace method directly using reflection to access private method
    // This is generally discouraged but useful for testing complex private logic.
    // For a production system, consider making this method internal and visible to the test assembly.
    private List<FreeSpace> CallSplitSpace(FreeSpace originalSpace, Box placedBox)
    {
        var method = typeof(BinPacker).GetMethod("SplitSpace", System.Reflection.BindingFlags.NonPublic | System.Reflection.BindingFlags.Instance);
        return (List<FreeSpace>)method.Invoke(_packer, new object[] { originalSpace, placedBox });
    }

    [Test]
    public void SplitSpace_BoxInCorner_GeneratesCorrectSpaces()
    {
        var originalSpace = new FreeSpace(0, 0, 0, 10, 10, 10);
        var placedBox = new Box("CornerBox", 5, 5, 5) { X = 0, Y = 0, Z = 0 };

        var newSpaces = CallSplitSpace(originalSpace, placedBox);

        Assert.That(newSpaces.Count, Is.EqualTo(3)); // Expect 3 spaces: right, above, in front

        // Space to the right (X-split)
        var spaceX = newSpaces.FirstOrDefault(s => s.X == 5 && s.Y == 0 && s.Z == 0);
        Assert.That(spaceX, Is.Not.Null);
        Assert.That(spaceX.Length, Is.EqualTo(5));
        Assert.That(spaceX.Width, Is.EqualTo(10));
        Assert.That(spaceX.Height, Is.EqualTo(10));

        // Space above (Y-split)
        var spaceY = newSpaces.FirstOrDefault(s => s.X == 0 && s.Y == 5 && s.Z == 0);
        Assert.That(spaceY, Is.Not.Null);
        Assert.That(spaceY.Length, Is.EqualTo(5));
        Assert.That(spaceY.Width, Is.EqualTo(5));
        Assert.That(spaceY.Height, Is.EqualTo(10));
        
        // Space in front (Z-split)
        var spaceZ = newSpaces.FirstOrDefault(s => s.X == 0 && s.Y == 0 && s.Z == 5);
        Assert.That(spaceZ, Is.Not.Null);
        Assert.That(spaceZ.Length, Is.EqualTo(5));
        Assert.That(spaceZ.Width, Is.EqualTo(5));
        Assert.That(spaceZ.Height, Is.EqualTo(5));
    }
    
    [Test]
    public void SplitSpace_BoxInMiddle_GeneratesCorrectSpaces()
    {
        var originalSpace = new FreeSpace(0, 0, 0, 10, 10, 10);
        var placedBox = new Box("MidBox", 4, 4, 4) { X = 3, Y = 3, Z = 3 };

        var newSpaces = CallSplitSpace(originalSpace, placedBox);

        // Expect 6 spaces: left, right, below, above, behind, in front
        // The implementation creates 6 spaces. Need to verify each.
        Assert.That(newSpaces.Count, Is.EqualTo(6)); 
        
        // Assertions for each of the 6 spaces
        // Space 1: "left" of the placed box (along X-axis)
        var space1 = newSpaces.FirstOrDefault(s => s.X == 0 && s.Y == 0 && s.Z == 0);
        Assert.That(space1.Length, Is.EqualTo(3));
        Assert.That(space1.Width, Is.EqualTo(10));
        Assert.That(space1.Height, Is.EqualTo(10));

        // Space 2: "right" of the placed box (along X-axis)
        var space2 = newSpaces.FirstOrDefault(s => s.X == 7 && s.Y == 0 && s.Z == 0);
        Assert.That(space2.Length, Is.EqualTo(3));
        Assert.That(space2.Width, Is.EqualTo(10));
        Assert.That(space2.Height, Is.EqualTo(10));

        // Space 3: "below" the placed box (along Y-axis, within the X-range of the box)
        var space3 = newSpaces.FirstOrDefault(s => s.X == 3 && s.Y == 0 && s.Z == 0);
        Assert.That(space3.Length, Is.EqualTo(4));
        Assert.That(space3.Width, Is.EqualTo(3));
        Assert.That(space3.Height, Is.EqualTo(10));

        // Space 4: "above" the placed box (along Y-axis, within the X-range of the box)
        var space4 = newSpaces.FirstOrDefault(s => s.X == 3 && s.Y == 7 && s.Z == 0);
        Assert.That(space4.Length, Is.EqualTo(4));
        Assert.That(space4.Width, Is.EqualTo(3));
        Assert.That(space4.Height, Is.EqualTo(10));

        // Space 5: "behind" the placed box (along Z-axis, within the X and Y ranges of the box)
        var space5 = newSpaces.FirstOrDefault(s => s.X == 3 && s.Y == 3 && s.Z == 0);
        Assert.That(space5.Length, Is.EqualTo(4));
        Assert.That(space5.Width, Is.EqualTo(4));
        Assert.That(space5.Height, Is.EqualTo(3));

        // Space 6: "in front" of the placed box (along Z-axis, within the X and Y ranges of the box)
        var space6 = newSpaces.FirstOrDefault(s => s.X == 3 && s.Y == 3 && s.Z == 7);
        Assert.That(space6.Length, Is.EqualTo(4));
        Assert.That(space6.Width, Is.EqualTo(4));
        Assert.That(space6.Height, Is.EqualTo(3));
    }
}

Test 18: Minimum Cut Problem (C++) Score: 0.00 / 9
Typical Prompt:
You are writing a C++ program to solve the Minimum Edge Cut problem.

**Target environment specs**
Platform: Windows 11 (build 10.0.26200)
Architecture: AMD64
CPU: AMD64 Family 23 Model 8 Stepping 2, AuthenticAMD
Core count: 64
Memory: 127.87 GB
SIMD: AVX, AVX2, SSE, SSE2, SSE3, SSE4.1, SSE4.2, SSSE3
SIMD detail: AVX=yes, AVX-512F=no, AVX2=yes, SSE=yes, SSE2=yes, SSE3=yes, SSE4.1=yes, SSE4.2=yes, SSSE3=yes

**C++ Compiler information**
MSVC cl 19.50.35730 | std=c++20 (/std:c++20)

You must write a C++ solver that can handle ANY graph size from trivial to multi-gb.

**The Challenge:**
Your C++ program will be tested with graphs ranging from small toy examples to massive real-world networks.
The same program must work efficiently across ALL scales.

**Problem:**
Given an undirected connected graph, find the minimum number of edges to remove
such that the graph becomes disconnected (split into two non-empty components).

**Input format (stdin):**
- Line 1: N M (number of nodes, number of edges)
- Lines 2 to M+1: u v (undirected edge between nodes u and v, 0-indexed)

**Output format (stdout):**
- Line 1: K (number of edges in the minimum cut)
- Lines 2 to K+1: u v (each edge in the cut, in any order)

**Requirements:**
1. Program must compile with this compiler, and run on this environment.
2. Read from stdin, write to stdout
3. Handle graphs even if they don't fit in memory.
4. Complete as fast as possible.
5. Output must be a valid cut that disconnects the graph
6. Minimize the number of cut edges
7. Must handle varying graph sizes efficiently

**Example:**
Input:
```
4 5
0 1
1 2
2 3
3 0
0 2
```

Output (one possible answer):
```
2
0 1
3 0
```

**Environment:**
Platform: Windows 11 (build 10.0.26200)
Architecture: AMD64
CPU: AMD64 Family 23 Model 8 Stepping 2, AuthenticAMD
Core count: 64
Memory: 127.87 GB
SIMD: AVX, AVX2, SSE, SSE2, SSE3, SSE4.1, SSE4.2, SSSE3
SIMD detail: AVX=yes, AVX-512F=no, AVX2=yes, SSE=yes, SSE2=yes, SSE3=yes, SSE4.1=yes, SSE4.2=yes, SSSE3=yes

**C++ Compiler:**
MSVC cl 19.50.35730 | std=c++20 (/std:c++20)

Be sure that any deviation from the C++ standard library is supported by the given compiler,
as referencing the wrong intrinsics or non-standard header like 'bits/stdc++.h' could fail your submission.

Write complete, compilable C++ code with a main() function.
Include adaptive logic that chooses different algorithms based on graph scale.
 Prompt Changes:
Prompt parameters change between subpasses (increasing difficulty)
Subpass 0

View chain of thought

Find the smallest set of edges to remove from a graph so that it splits into two disconnected pieces. Think of it like finding the weakest point in a network — the fewest cables you’d need to cut to divide it in two.

Subpasses increase the graph size from small networks to very large ones. The AI must implement an efficient algorithm; naïve approaches are far too slow on the bigger inputs.

Test 19: 3D Voxel Mining (C#) Score: 0.00 / 13
Typical Prompt:
You are writing a C# program to solve a mining problem in a simplified 3D voxel space.

**Constraints:**
1. Can only dig dirt that is exposed from above or a side.
2. Dug dirt must be dumped somewhere - at a location not blocking future digging
3. Maximum uphill slope - 1 Z voxel per X/Y voxel.
4. Dumped dirt becomes a new obstacle (plan carefully!)
5. Goal is to get valuable ore to a processing plant located at x=0, y=0
6. Ground is stable and solid, and you can dig under any voxel without fear of it collapsing
so long as it's connected to the earth. If you disconnect a voxel(s), it will collapse.

The voxel grid size has 10x more precision in Z than X/Y. So 10x10x100 is a cube.

**Input header format (stdin - space delimited. Ends with newline):**
```
XVoxelCount YVoxelCount ZVoxelCount XBlockSize YBlockSize ZBlockSize
```
Counts are human readable integers. BlockSize are human readable decimal numbers.

After the newline, voxels then follow in bytes, like this python psuedocode:
```
for x in range(XVoxelCount):
for y in range(YVoxelCount):
for z in range(ZVoxelCount):
d = readByte(stdin)
massInTonnes = d % 16
valuePerTonne = d // 16
addVoxel(d)
```

Each peice of dirt needs to be moved to its new location - you need to trace the
path in X and Y (4-connected / manhatten steps) and ensure that the path doesn't climb uphill
or downhill more than 1 Z voxel for every X/Y voxel.

**Output format (stdout / newline seperated):**
```
M (number of operations)
N digX digY digZ stepX1 stepY1 stepX2 stepY2 (N steps) destX destY (for each operation)
```

If dumping valuable ore at the processing plant, the voxel ceases to exist. If a voxel is
dumped elsewhere, it is dumped on top of the ground, raising the ground by 1 voxel. It can
be dug again and moved later if required.

Every unit of mass requires $1 of fuel to move one voxel in X or Y when flat, or $5 of fuel
to go up a 10% slope. Downhill is free.

Getting valuable ore to your processing plant pays $1000 per unit of mass per unit of value.
The most valuable ore (#15) pays $15,000 per ton, so the most a single dump truck load can gross
is $225,000.

(Dumping junk ore at the processing plant causes damage to the plant, costing $1000 per ton.)

**Requirements:**
1. Program must compile with .NET/csc
2. Read from stdin, write to stdout
3. World size could be anything from 10x10x10 to 5000x5000x5000, your code should handle both extremes.
4. Your code should complete in under 5 minutes
5. Maximize profit.

**Environment:**
Platform: Windows 11 (build 10.0.26200)
Architecture: AMD64
CPU: AMD64 Family 23 Model 8 Stepping 2, AuthenticAMD
Core count: 64
Memory: 127.87 GB
SIMD: AVX, AVX2, SSE, SSE2, SSE3, SSE4.1, SSE4.2, SSSE3
SIMD detail: AVX=yes, AVX-512F=no, AVX2=yes, SSE=yes, SSE2=yes, SSE3=yes, SSE4.1=yes, SSE4.2=yes, SSSE3=yes

**C# Compiler:**
C# (csc.exe 5.5.0-2.26208.13 (3dc781932f83b0792b0d1717a68db3f9604a2cb1)) | max LangVersion 14


Write complete, compilable C# code with a Main method.
Include adaptive logic that chooses different strategies based on problem scale.
 Prompt Changes:
Prompt parameters change between subpasses (increasing difficulty)
Subpass 0
Parameters: (see typical prompt)View exact prompt
View raw AI output
View chain of thought

Plan an open-pit mine in a 3D block model. Each block has a value (ore worth money, waste costs money to move) and you can only dig a block once the blocks above it have been removed. The goal is to maximise profit by choosing which blocks to extract and where to dump the waste.

The AI must balance revenue against haulage costs, respect slope constraints for road access, and avoid dumping waste where it blocks future digging. Subpasses increase the pit size and complexity.

Test 20: Drillhole Data Validation (Rust) Score: 0.00 / 14
Typical Prompt:
You are writing a Rust program to validate drillhole assay data.


**The Challenge:**
Your Rust program will be tested with drillhole datasets ranging from small exploration projects to
massive mining surveys. The same program must work efficiently across ALL scales from 10 holes to
200,000 holes.

**Problem:**
Given drillhole data from a mining exploration project, identify samples that likely contain typos or
measurement errors. These outliers don't correlate well with spatially neighboring data points.

**Target environment specs**
Platform: Windows 11 (build 10.0.26200)
Architecture: AMD64
CPU: AMD64 Family 23 Model 8 Stepping 2, AuthenticAMD
Core count: 64
Memory: 127.87 GB
SIMD: AVX, AVX2, SSE, SSE2, SSE3, SSE4.1, SSE4.2, SSSE3
SIMD detail: AVX=yes, AVX-512F=no, AVX2=yes, SSE=yes, SSE2=yes, SSE3=yes, SSE4.1=yes, SSE4.2=yes, SSSE3=yes

**Rust Compiler information**
rustc 1.92.0 (ded5c06cf 2025-12-08) | edition=2024 | host: x86_64-pc-windows-msvc

**Drillhole Data:**
- Each hole has a 3D path: start point (x,y,z) + direction vector + length
- Samples are taken at intervals along each hole
- Each sample has measurements for multiple properties

**Input format (stdin):**
```
num_holes num_properties
property_name_1 property_name_2 ...
hole_id start_x start_y start_z dir_x dir_y dir_z length num_samples
depth prop1 prop2 ...
depth prop1 prop2 ...
etc.
hole_id start_x start_y start_z dir_x dir_y dir_z length num_samples
```

**Output format (stdout):**
```
M (number of suspect entries found)
hole_id sample_idx property_name confidence (for each suspect, sorted by confidence desc)
```

**Requirements:**
1. Program must compile with rustc and only the standard rust library.
2. Read from stdin, write to stdout
3. Handle up to thousands of holes with thousands of samples each
4. Complete within 5 minutes
5. Report at least the most obvious outliers
6. Confidence score should reflect how anomalous the value is
7. Must handle varying dataset sizes efficiently

Write complete, compilable Rust code with a main function.
Include adaptive logic that chooses different strategies based on dataset scale.

The program should be robust enough to handle edge cases like missing data, duplicate entries, and varying data distributions.
 Prompt Changes:
Prompt parameters change between subpasses (increasing difficulty)
Subpass 0
Parameters: (see typical prompt)View exact prompt
View raw AI output
View chain of thought

Given a set of mining drillholes — each a 3D path with measurements taken at intervals along its length — identify the data entries that are most likely to be errors (typos, instrument glitches, or transcription mistakes).

The AI must compare each measurement against its spatial neighbours: a reading that disagrees with nearby drillholes is suspect. Subpasses increase the number of drillholes and properties, requiring efficient spatial reasoning at scale.

Test 21: N-Dimensional Snake Game (C++) Score: 0.00 / 11
Typical Prompt:
You are writing C++ code to play an N-Dimensional Snake Game.

You must write a C++ solver that can handle ANY dimensional complexity from trivial to ludicrous scale:
- **Trivial**: 2D games (eg 8x8), few obstacles, basic pathfinding
- **Medium**: 3D-4D games (eg 6x6x6 to 8x8x8x8), moderate obstacles, intermediate planning
- **Large**: 6D-8D games (eg 4x4x4x4x4x4 to 6x6x6x6x6x6x6x6), many obstacles, complex pathfinding
- **Extreme**: 10D-20D games (eg 3x3x3... to 4x4x4...)

**The Challenge:**
Your C++ snake player will be tested with games ranging from simple 2D boards to massive 20D hypercubes.
The same algorithm must work efficiently across ALL dimensionalities.

**Problem:**
Like classic Nokia snake, but generalized to arbitrary dimensions. The snake occupies a series of
connected integer grid points in N-D space, moves one step per turn along one axis (+1 or -1),
grows when eating food, and dies if it hits an obstacle or itself.

**Input format (stdin, whitespace / newline seperated):**
```
D max_turns (D = number of dimensions, max_turns = turn limit)
bounds (D integers: size of space in each dimension)
snake_length start_pos (length, then D integers for head position)
F (number of food items)
F lines: food positions (D integers each)
O (number of obstacles)
O lines: obstacle positions (D integers each)
```

**Output format (stdout):**
Each turn, output: axis direction
- axis: 0 to D-1 (which dimension to move along)
- direction: -1 or +1 (which way to move)

**Game ends when:**
- Snake eats all food (win)
- Snake hits obstacle or itself (lose)
- Max turns reached

**Environment:**
Platform: Windows 11 (build 10.0.26200)
Architecture: AMD64
CPU: AMD64 Family 23 Model 8 Stepping 2, AuthenticAMD
Core count: 64
Memory: 127.87 GB
SIMD: AVX, AVX2, SSE, SSE2, SSE3, SSE4.1, SSE4.2, SSSE3
SIMD detail: AVX=yes, AVX-512F=no, AVX2=yes, SSE=yes, SSE2=yes, SSE3=yes, SSE4.1=yes, SSE4.2=yes, SSSE3=yes

**C++ Compiler:**
MSVC cl 19.50.35730 | std=c++20 (/std:c++20)

Be sure that any deviation from the C++ standard library is supported by the given compiler,
as referencing the wrong intrinsics or non-standard header like 'bits/stdc++.h' could fail your submission.

Write complete, compilable C++ code with a main() function.
 Prompt Changes:
Prompt parameters change between subpasses (increasing difficulty)
Subpass 0
Parameters: (see typical prompt)View exact prompt
View raw AI output
View chain of thought

Play the classic Snake game, but in more than two dimensions. The snake moves through an N-dimensional grid, eating food to grow while avoiding its own body and the walls. In higher dimensions the grid is enormous but self-collision becomes far trickier to reason about.

Subpasses increase the number of dimensions, making the state space explode. The AI must write a strategy that plans a safe path to food without boxing itself in.

Test 22: Tetris Game (C#) Score: 0.00 / 11
Typical Prompt:
You are writing C# code to play Tetris optimally.

Your C# Tetris player will be tested with games ranging from short practice sessions to marathon games.

**Problem:**
Standard Tetris rules apply - variable width board (typically 10-20 columns),
pieces fall from top, clear lines by filling them completely, game over when pieces stack to the top.
The program receives pieces one at a time and must decide optimal placement.

**Input format (stdin, whitespace seperated decimal integers):**
```
width height num_pieces
For each piece:
Line: piece_type (I, O, T, S, Z, J, L)
Program outputs: rotation column
Then receives: result (ok, cleared_N, gameover)
```

**Output format (stdout, 1 line per piece, whitespace seperated decimal integers):**
rotation column
- rotation: 0-3 (number of 90° clockwise rotations)
- column: 0 to width-1 (leftmost column of piece)

**Piece Types:**
- I: 4x1 line piece
- O: 2x2 square piece
- T: T-shaped piece
- S: S-shaped piece (right-leaning)
- Z: Z-shaped piece (left-leaning)
- J: L-shaped piece (left-leaning)
- L: L-shaped piece (right-leaning)

**Requirements:**
1. Program must compile with .NET/csc
2. Read from stdin, write to stdout
3. Handle variable board widths and long game sequences
4. Must place a peice within 5 seconds of it being written to STDIN.

**Environment:**
Platform: Windows 11 (build 10.0.26200)
Architecture: AMD64
CPU: AMD64 Family 23 Model 8 Stepping 2, AuthenticAMD
Core count: 64
Memory: 127.87 GB
SIMD: AVX, AVX2, SSE, SSE2, SSE3, SSE4.1, SSE4.2, SSSE3
SIMD detail: AVX=yes, AVX-512F=no, AVX2=yes, SSE=yes, SSE2=yes, SSE3=yes, SSE4.1=yes, SSE4.2=yes, SSSE3=yes

**C# Compiler:**
C# (csc.exe 5.5.0-2.26208.13 (3dc781932f83b0792b0d1717a68db3f9604a2cb1)) | max LangVersion 14


Write complete, compilable C# code with a Main method.
Include adaptive logic that chooses different strategies based on game complexity.
 Prompt Changes:
Prompt parameters change between subpasses (increasing difficulty)
Subpass 0
Parameters: (see typical prompt)View exact prompt
View raw AI output
View chain of thought

Write an AI that plays Tetris. Given the current board state and the next piece, choose where to place it to survive as long as possible and clear as many lines as you can. Good play means keeping the board flat, avoiding buried holes, and setting up multi-line clears.

Subpasses increase the board size and speed, demanding smarter lookahead and evaluation. The AI must balance short-term survival with long-term board health.

Test 23: Lunar Lander Game Score: 0.00 / 11
Typical Prompt:
You are writing Rust code to play Lunar Lander in real-time.

Your Rust lunar lander controller will be tested with scenarios ranging from simple
training environments to very complex landing sites.

Control a lunar lander to safely land on a target pad.
The program receives a 2D voxel map and continuous state updates, and must output thrust/turn
commands to land safely on the target.

Data is streamed to stdin in real time, and you must write to stdout in real time. If your code
doesn't read from stdin fast enough and the io buffer fills up, the ship will likely crash.

Be very careful if writing single threaded / blocking code, this has many failure modes that
async code doesn't have. Multithreaded / asyncio code is HIGHLY recommended.

**Physics:**
- Gravity pulls lander down constantly
- Thrust applies force in lander's facing direction
- Fuel is limited
- Must land softly on target (low velocity)

**Input format (stdin, whitespace seperated decimal numbers):**
Initial map:
```
width height gravity max_thrust fuel
start_x start_y target_x target_y
map rows (. = empty, # = obstacle, S = start, T = target, L = landing zone)
```

Then you recieve continuous state updates (every 0.1 seconds):
```
x y vx vy angle angular_vel fuel_remaining
```
x and y are in meters, from the worlds top left, and y increases downward.
vx and vy are in m/s.
angle is in radians, 0 is facing up.
angular_vel is in radians per second. + is clockwise, - is counterclockwise.
fuel_remaining is seconds at max_thrust.

**Output format (stdout):**
Your code needs to output the following instructions at a frequency of at least 20hz:
```
thrust turn
```
- thrust: 0.0 to 1.0 (fraction of max thrust)
- turn: -1.0 to 1.0 (turning rate, negative = left, positive = right)

To be crystal clear: that's two decimal numbers seperated by a space, followed by a newline.

If your code stalls / freezes for 100ms or more (so it misses 2 consecutive updates), the engine
will automatically cut power. A long freeze and eventually the lander will crash.

Map size can vary from 100x50 to multi-gb maps, so be efficient with memory and processing.

Thrust and gravity are in m/s/s. 100% engine burn burns 1 unit of fuel per second.

Lander size is approximatly 1x1m, and the 2D map grid size is 1m x 1m.

Turn is in radians per second. The lander can not rotate fully around and will clamp rotation to
facing down if attempted.

Y increases downward. Positive vy means moving downward. Gravity is +g.

**Success Conditions:**
- Land on target with |velocity| < 2m/s
- Stay within landing zone boundaries
- Complete landing within time limit

**Failure Conditions:**
- Crash into obstacle or ground too hard
- Run out of fuel
- Exceed time limit

**Environment:**
Platform: Windows 11 (build 10.0.26200)
Architecture: AMD64

Write complete, compilable Rust code with a main function. Assume only the standard library is available.
 Prompt Changes:
Prompt parameters change between subpasses (increasing difficulty)
Subpass 0
Parameters: (see typical prompt)View exact prompt
View raw AI output
View chain of thought

Land a spacecraft softly on the Moon. The lander starts above the surface with some velocity, and the AI must control the main thruster and side jets to touch down gently — too fast and it crashes, too much fuel wasted and it runs dry.

Subpasses vary the starting altitude, velocity, and terrain. The AI must handle 2D physics, fuel management, and real-time control to achieve a safe landing.

Test 24: 3D Lunar Lander Game (C++) Score: 0.00 / 10
Typical Prompt:
You are writing C++ code to play a 3D Lunar Lander game in real-time.

Your C++ controller will be tested with scenarios ranging from tiny (100m) to huge (100km).
The same code must work for all scales.

Control a lunar lander in full 3D space to safely land on a target pad.
The program receives a terrain file path and continuous state updates via stdin, and must
output thrust/rotation commands to stdout in real-time.

Data is streamed to stdin in real time, and you must write to stdout in real time.
If your code doesn't read from stdin fast enough and the IO buffer fills up, the ship
will likely crash.

Be very careful if writing single threaded / blocking code, this has many failure modes
that async code doesn't have. Multithreaded code is HIGHLY recommended.

**Coordinate System:**
- X: horizontal (East), Y: horizontal (North), Z: UP.
- Gravity acts in the -Z direction.
- All positions are in meters. Velocities in m/s. Angles in radians.

**Physics:**
- Gravity = 1.62 m/s² (lunar) in -Z direction.
- Thrust is along the lander's local "up" axis. When pitch=0 and yaw=0, thrust is
straight up (+Z). Tilting via pitch/yaw redirects thrust.
- Pitch: tilts the lander. +pitch tilts forward, redirecting thrust partially into -X.
Pitch is clamped to [-pi/2, pi/2].
- Yaw: rotates heading around Z.
- Roll: rolls around the thrust axis (cosmetic only, does not affect thrust direction).
- Rotation dynamics: commands apply angular acceleration (2 rad/s² per unit).
Angular rates are damped by 0.95 per tick.
- Fuel: 100% thrust burns 1 unit of fuel per second.

**Input format (stdin):**
Initial header (whitespace separated decimal numbers, except terrain path):
```
width depth height gravity max_thrust fuel
start_x start_y start_z target_x target_y target_z
terrain_file_path
STATE
```

The terrain_file_path is on its own line (the path may contain spaces).
Read the entire line with getline() or equivalent.

**Terrain file format (binary):**
The terrain file defines the 3D world as a binary file. It is a sequence of
uint16_t triplets (little-endian), one per (x, y) cell, stored in row-major order:

```cpp
for (int x = 0; x < width; x++) {
for (int y = 0; y < depth; y++) {
uint16_t a = read_uint16_le(); // ground rock top
uint16_t b = read_uint16_le(); // floating rock bottom
uint16_t c = read_uint16_le(); // floating rock top
}
}
```

For each column (x, y), the vertical structure is:
- z < a: SOLID ROCK (ground)
- a <= z < b: OPEN AIR
- b <= z < c: SOLID ROCK (floating terrain feature, overhang, arch, etc.)
- c <= z: OPEN AIR

If b == c, there is no floating rock in that column (just ground + open sky).
The file size is exactly width * depth * 6 bytes.

You have a startup grace period to parse this file before state updates begin.
The grace period is 5 seconds plus 10 seconds per gigabyte of terrain file.
For a 100x100 map this is ~5s. For a 100000x10000 map (~6GB) this is ~65s.
You may continue parsing in a background thread after the simulation begins,
but you MUST be reading stdin and writing stdout by then to avoid a crash.

Then continuous state updates (every 0.1 seconds, whitespace separated):
```
x y z vx vy vz pitch yaw roll pitch_rate yaw_rate roll_rate fuel_remaining
```

**Output format (stdout):**
Your code needs to output the following at a frequency of at least 10hz:
```
thrust pitch_cmd yaw_cmd roll_cmd
```
- thrust: 0.0 to 1.0 (fraction of max_thrust)
- pitch_cmd: -1.0 to 1.0 (pitch angular acceleration command)
- yaw_cmd: -1.0 to 1.0 (yaw angular acceleration command)
- roll_cmd: -1.0 to 1.0 (roll angular acceleration command)

To be crystal clear: four decimal numbers separated by spaces, followed by a newline.

If your code stalls / freezes for 100ms or more (misses 2 consecutive updates), the
engine will cut thrust automatically. A long freeze and eventually the lander will crash.

**Landing:**
- The target is at (target_x, target_y, target_z) which is just above the terrain.
- You must land within a small radius of the target with total speed < 3 m/s.
- Landing zone radius is approximately max(5, min(width,depth)*0.02) meters.

**Terrain collisions:**
- You collide if you enter any solid cell as defined by the terrain file.
- Out of bounds (x<0, x>=width, y<0, y>=depth, z<0, z>=height) is also a collision.

**Success Conditions:**
- Land on target with |velocity| < 3 m/s
- Stay within landing zone boundaries

**Failure Conditions:**
- Crash into terrain or ground too hard
- Run out of fuel
- Exceed simulation time limit

**Environment:**
Platform: Windows 11 (build 10.0.26200)
Architecture: AMD64
CPU: AMD64 Family 23 Model 8 Stepping 2, AuthenticAMD
Core count: 64
Memory: 127.87 GB
SIMD: AVX, AVX2, SSE, SSE2, SSE3, SSE4.1, SSE4.2, SSSE3
SIMD detail: AVX=yes, AVX-512F=no, AVX2=yes, SSE=yes, SSE2=yes, SSE3=yes, SSE4.1=yes, SSE4.2=yes, SSSE3=yes

**C++ Compiler:**
MSVC cl 19.50.35730 | std=c++20 (/std:c++20)

Write complete, compilable C++ code with a main() function.

Be sure that any deviation from the C++ standard library is supported by the given compiler,
as referencing the wrong intrinsics or non-standard header like 'bits/stdc++.h' could fail your submission.

 Prompt Changes:
Prompt parameters change between subpasses (increasing difficulty)

Land a spacecraft on a 3D lunar surface with full attitude control. Unlike the 2D version, the lander can pitch, yaw, and roll, and the terrain is a detailed 3D heightmap with floating rock obstacles. The AI must parse a binary terrain file and navigate to a safe touchdown.

Subpasses vary the terrain complexity, starting conditions, and obstacle density. The AI must handle 6-degree-of-freedom physics and real-time control in three dimensions.
